narrative:
In the next place, dealings between man and man require to be suitably regulated. The principle of them is very simple: Thou shalt not, if thou canst help, touch that which is mine, or remove the least thing which belongs to me without my consent; and may I be of a sound mind, and do to others as I would that they should do to me.

First, let us speak of treasure trove: May I never pray the Gods to find the hidden treasure, which another has laid up for himself and his family, he not being one of my ancestors, nor lift, if I should find, such a treasure. And may I never have any dealings with those who are called diviners, and who in any way or manner counsel me to take up the deposit entrusted to the earth, for I should not gain so much in the increase of my possessions, if I take up the prize, as I should grow in justice and virtue of soul, if I abstain; and this will be a better possession to me than the other in a better part of myself; for the possession of justice in the soul is preferable to the possession of wealth.

And of many things it is well said, "Move not the immovables," and this may be regarded as one of them. And we shall do well to believe the common tradition which says that such deeds prevent a man from having a family.
refashioned:
Next, we need proper regulations for dealings between people. The underlying principle is straightforward: you must not, if you can help it, touch what belongs to me, nor take even the smallest thing of mine without my permission; and I pray I remain of sound mind, treating others as I would wish to be treated.

First, let us address the matter of found treasure. May I never pray to the gods that I discover hidden wealth laid up by someone else for himself and his family -- someone who is not my ancestor -- nor pick up such treasure if I happen to find it. And may I never consult those so-called diviners who advise me to dig up deposits entrusted to the earth. For the gain in material wealth from seizing such a prize would be far less than the growth in justice and virtue I would achieve by leaving it alone. Justice in the soul is a better possession, held in a better part of myself, than any material wealth could be.

As the old saying goes, "Do not disturb what is settled" -- and this situation is a prime example. We would also do well to credit the common tradition that taking what is not yours prevents a man from having descendants.
elegant: Dealings between people rest on a simple principle: do not touch what belongs to another without consent, and treat others as you would wish to be treated. Found treasure should be left alone -- the growth in justice from abstaining far exceeds any material gain, for possessing justice in the soul outweighs possessing wealth. The old saying applies: do not disturb what is settled. Tradition warns that taking what is not yours prevents a man from having descendants.
summary: The Athenian Stranger introduces the laws governing private dealings with a golden-rule principle: do not take what belongs to another. Found treasure must be left untouched, because the spiritual gain from justice far outweighs any material prize, and tradition warns that such theft prevents one from having descendants.
distilled: The foundation of all commercial law is simple: do not take what is not yours, for justice in the soul is worth more than any material wealth.
notes: The Athenian Stranger speaks, introducing Book 11's treatment of commercial and property law. -- The golden-rule formulation ('do to others as I would that they should do to me') is one of the earliest expressions of this principle in Western philosophy, predating its more famous biblical articulations. -- 'Move not the immovables' was a proverbial saying attributed to Pythagoras and widely circulated in Greek wisdom literature. -- The idea that justice in the soul is preferable to external wealth echoes Republic IV (443c-444a), where Socrates argues that justice is the proper ordering of the soul's parts. -- The belief that theft prevents one from having descendants reflects Greek religious tradition about divine punishment extending across generations, found also in Solon's poetry and Herodotus. -- The 'Goddess of ways' mentioned in the next passage is Hecate, protectress of crossroads and wayside property.

narrative:
Now as to him who is careless about having children and regardless of the legislator, taking up that which neither he deposited, nor any ancestor of his, without the consent of the depositor, violating the simplest and noblest of laws which was the enactment of no mean man -- "Take not up that which was not laid down by thee" -- of him, I say, who despises these two legislators, and takes up, not small matter which he has not deposited, but perhaps a great heap of treasure, what he ought to suffer at the hands of the Gods, God only knows; but I would have the first person who sees him go and tell the wardens of the city, if the occurrence has taken place in the city, or if the occurrence has taken place in the agora he shall tell the wardens of the agora, or if in the country he shall tell the wardens of the country and their commanders.

When information has been received the city shall send to Delphi, and, whatever the God answers about the money and the remover of the money, that the city shall do in obedience to the oracle; the informer, if he be a freeman, shall have the honour of doing rightly, and he who informs not, the dishonour of doing wrongly; and if he be a slave who gives information, let him be freed, as he ought to be, by the state, which shall give his master the price of him; but if he do not inform he shall be punished with death.

Next in order shall follow a similar law, which shall apply equally to matters great and small: If a man happens to leave behind him some part of his property, whether intentionally or unintentionally, let him who may come upon the left property suffer it to remain, reflecting that such things are under the protection of the Goddess of ways, and are dedicated to her by the law.
refashioned:
As for the person who cares nothing about having children and ignores the legislator -- who takes up what neither he nor any ancestor of his deposited, without the depositor's consent, violating that simplest and noblest of laws enacted by no ordinary man, "Do not take up what you did not lay down" -- this person who scorns both divine and human lawgivers, and who takes not some trifle but perhaps a great heap of treasure: what punishment he deserves from the gods, only God knows. But I require that the first witness to such an act report it to the wardens of the city if it occurred in the city, to the wardens of the agora if in the marketplace, or to the wardens and commanders of the countryside if it occurred in the country.

Once the report is received, the city shall send an inquiry to Delphi, and whatever the god commands regarding the money and the person who took it, the city shall carry out in obedience to the oracle. A free informer shall receive the honor of having done right, while one who fails to report shall bear the dishonor of having done wrong. If a slave provides the information, the state shall free him and compensate his master at fair price; but if a slave witnesses the act and does not report it, he shall be punished with death.

A similar law follows, applying equally to matters great and small: if someone leaves behind any part of his property, whether on purpose or by accident, whoever comes across it should leave it where it is, understanding that such things are under the protection of the Goddess of the Ways and are consecrated to her by law.
elegant: Anyone who takes treasure he did not deposit, violating the ancient law "Do not take what you did not lay down," faces divine judgment. The first witness must report the act to the appropriate wardens. The city shall consult the oracle at Delphi and obey its ruling. Free informers earn honor; those who fail to report earn dishonor. A slave who informs shall be freed at public expense; a slave who stays silent shall die. Similarly, any property left behind, whether intentionally or accidentally, must be left untouched -- it is under the protection of the Goddess of the Ways.
summary: Those who take found treasure face divine punishment, and the law requires witnesses to report such acts to the appropriate wardens, with the city consulting the Delphic oracle for judgment. Informers are rewarded and silence is punished. All abandoned property is sacred to Hecate and must be left alone.
distilled: Found treasure must be reported to the wardens and judged by the Delphic oracle, with rewards for informers and severe penalties for silence.
notes: The Athenian Stranger continues the treatment of found property. -- The law 'Take not up that which was not laid down by thee' is attributed to an unnamed ancient lawgiver, possibly Solon or one of the Seven Sages. -- The three-tiered system of wardens (city, agora, country) reflects the administrative structure proposed throughout the Laws for the colony of Magnesia. -- The consultation of Delphi for legal decisions was a real Greek practice; Delphi served as an advisory authority on matters of religious and civil law. -- The 'Goddess of ways' is Hecate, who was worshipped at crossroads and believed to protect travelers and wayside property. -- The provision freeing informant slaves has parallels in Athenian legal practice, where slaves who provided valuable information could receive their freedom. -- The death penalty for a silent slave reflects the harsh realities of Greek attitudes toward slave obligations.

narrative:
But if any one defies the law, and takes the property home with him, let him, if the thing is of little worth, and the man who takes it a slave, be beaten with many stripes by him, being a person of not less than thirty years of age. Or if he be a freeman, in addition to being thought a mean person and a despiser of the laws, let him pay ten times the value of the treasure which he has moved to the leaver.

And if some one accuses another of having anything which belongs to him, whether little or much, and the other admits that he has this thing, but denies that the property in dispute belongs to other, if the property be registered with the magistrates according to law, the claimant shall summon the possessor, who shall bring it before the magistrates; and when it is brought into court, if it be registered in the public registers, to which of the litigants it belonged, let him take it and go his way. Or if the property be registered as belonging to some one who is not present, whoever will offer sufficient surety on behalf of the absent person that he will give it up to him, shall take it away as the representative of the other.

But if the property which is deposited be not registered with the magistrates, let it remain until the time of trial with three of the eldest of the magistrates; and if it be an animal which is deposited, then he who loses the suit shall pay the magistrates for its keep, and they shall determine the cause within three days.
refashioned:
If anyone defies the law and takes the found property home, the penalty depends on status. A slave who takes something of little value shall be beaten with many stripes by any person at least thirty years old. A freeman who does the same, besides earning a reputation as a petty and lawless person, must pay ten times the value of what he took to the original owner.

If someone accuses another of possessing his property, whether the amount is great or small, and the possessor admits having the item but denies it belongs to the accuser, the following procedure applies. If the property is registered with the magistrates as required by law, the claimant shall summon the possessor to bring it before the magistrates. If the public registers show who the rightful owner is, that person shall take the property and be done with it. If the property is registered to someone who is absent, anyone who offers sufficient surety that he will deliver it to the absent owner may take it as that person's representative.

If the disputed property is not registered with the magistrates, it shall remain in the custody of the three eldest magistrates until the trial. If the property in question is a living animal, the losing party shall pay the magistrates for its upkeep, and the magistrates must resolve the case within three days.
elegant: A slave who takes found property of small value shall be beaten; a freeman must pay ten times its value and bear the disgrace. In property disputes, if the item is publicly registered, the registers determine ownership. If it belongs to an absent owner, a surety may claim it on their behalf. Unregistered property stays with the three eldest magistrates until trial. For living animals in custody, the loser pays upkeep, and the case must be resolved within three days.
summary: Penalties for taking found property are severe: slaves face corporal punishment, freemen pay ten times the value. Property disputes are resolved through public registers, surety arrangements for absent owners, or magistrate custody, with animal cases requiring resolution within three days.
distilled: Slaves who take found property face beatings and freemen pay tenfold restitution, while disputes over claimed property are resolved through public registration records and magistrate oversight.
notes: The Athenian Stranger details penalties and dispute-resolution procedures. -- The tenfold penalty for freemen was exceptionally harsh by Greek standards, reflecting the seriousness attached to property violations in the Laws. -- The age requirement of thirty for administering corporal punishment to slaves ensures a degree of maturity and authority in the punisher. -- The system of public property registers (grammata demosia) reflects actual Athenian administrative practices, though the Laws envisions a more systematic version. -- The three-day deadline for animal cases is a practical measure to limit the costs and complications of maintaining livestock in custody. -- The surety system (engue) was a well-established feature of Greek legal procedure, used throughout Athenian law for bail, guardianship, and property claims.

narrative:
Any one who is of sound mind may arrest his own slave, and do with him whatever he will of such things as are lawful; and he may arrest the runaway slave of any of his friends or kindred with a view to his safe-keeping. And if any one takes away him who is being carried off as a slave, intending to liberate him, he who is carrying him off shall let him go; but he who takes him away shall give three sufficient sureties; and if he give them, and not without giving them, he may take him away, but if he take him away after any other manner he shall be deemed guilty of violence, and being convicted shall pay as a penalty double the amount of the damages claimed to him who has been deprived of the slave.

Any man may also carry off a freedman, if he do not pay respect or sufficient respect to him who freed him. Now the respect shall be, that the freedman go three times in the month to the hearth of the person who freed him and offer to do whatever he ought, so far as he can; and he shall agree to make such a marriage as his former master approves. He shall not be permitted to have more property than he who gave him liberty, and what more he has shall belong to his master.
refashioned:
Any person of sound mind may arrest his own slave and do whatever lawful measures he sees fit. He may also detain the runaway slave of a friend or relative for safekeeping. If someone intervenes to take away a person being carried off as a slave, intending to free that person, the one carrying the slave must release him -- but the one taking him away must provide three adequate sureties. Only with these sureties may he take the person away. If he removes the person by any other means, he shall be convicted of violence and must pay double the damages claimed by the person deprived of the slave.

Additionally, any man may recapture a freedman who fails to show proper respect to the person who freed him. The required respect consists of the following: the freedman must visit the hearth of his former master three times each month and offer to perform whatever service he reasonably can. He must also agree to marry only with his former master's approval. Furthermore, the freedman may not possess more property than the person who gave him his liberty; any excess belongs to his former master.
elegant: A person of sound mind may arrest his own slave or detain a friend's runaway. Anyone claiming a person is wrongly enslaved must provide three sureties before removing him; taking him by force incurs double damages. A freedman who fails to show proper respect to his former master -- visiting his hearth three times monthly, offering service, and seeking approval for marriage -- may be recaptured. No freedman may possess more property than the person who freed him.
summary: The law governs slave arrest, contested enslavement (requiring sureties), and freedman obligations. Freedmen must visit their former master's hearth three times monthly, marry with his approval, and own no more property than he does, or face recapture.
distilled: Slave owners may arrest their own or friends' slaves, contested cases require sureties, and freedmen owe specific ongoing obligations of respect and economic subordination to their former masters.
notes: The Athenian Stranger addresses slave law and freedman obligations. -- The surety requirement for those claiming wrongful enslavement reflects a concern for due process even within a slave-owning society. -- The freedman's obligation to visit his patron's hearth (hestia) three times monthly parallels the Roman institution of patronage (patrocinium), where freedmen (liberti) owed ongoing duties (obsequium, operae) to their former masters (patroni). -- The property ceiling for freedmen -- they may not be wealthier than their former masters -- is unique to the Laws and reflects Plato's broader concern with economic inequality threatening social harmony, developed extensively in Books V and VI. -- These provisions should be read alongside the Laws' general treatment of slavery, which, while accepting the institution, is more moderate than much of Greek practice. Compare Aristotle's Politics I.4-7, where slavery is defended as natural.

narrative:
The freedman shall not remain in the state more than twenty years, but like other foreigners shall go away, taking his entire property with him, unless he has the consent of the magistrates and of his former master to remain. If a freedman or any other stranger has a property greater than the census of the third class, at the expiration of thirty days from the day on which this comes to pass, he shall take that which is his and go his way, and in this case he shall not be allowed to remain any longer by the magistrates.

And if any one disobeys this regulation, and is brought into court and convicted, he shall be punished with death, his property shall be confiscated. Suits about these matters shall take place before the tribes, unless the plaintiff and defendant have got rid of the accusation either before their neighbours or before judges chosen by them.

If a man lay claim to any animal or anything else which he declares to be his, let the possessor refer to the seller or to some honest and trustworthy person, who has given, or in some legitimate way made over the property to him; if he be a citizen or a metic, sojourning in the city, within thirty days, or, if the property have been delivered to him by a stranger, within five months, of which the middle month shall include the summer solstice.

When goods are exchanged by selling and buying, a man shall deliver them, and receive the price of them, at a fixed place in the agora, and have done with the matter; but he shall not buy or sell anywhere else, nor give credit.
refashioned:
A freedman may not remain in the state for more than twenty years. Like other foreigners, he must depart and take all his property with him -- unless both the magistrates and his former master consent to his staying. If a freedman or any other foreigner comes to possess property exceeding the census of the third property class, he has thirty days from that point to collect what is his and leave. In this case, the magistrates have no authority to let him stay.

Anyone who disobeys this regulation and is convicted in court shall be punished with death and have his property confiscated. Such cases shall be heard before the tribal courts, unless both parties resolve the matter beforehand, either through their neighbors or through mutually chosen judges.

If someone claims that an animal or any other item in another's possession is his, the possessor must refer the claimant to the person who sold or legitimately transferred the property to him. Citizens and resident aliens must do this within thirty days; if the property was received from a foreigner, the time limit is five months, with the middle month encompassing the summer solstice.

When goods are bought and sold, the exchange -- both delivery and payment -- must take place at a designated spot in the agora, and the transaction ends there. No buying or selling may occur elsewhere, and no credit shall be extended.
elegant: Freedmen may reside in the state for no more than twenty years without special permission. Any foreigner whose wealth exceeds the third class census must leave within thirty days or face death and confiscation. Property claims require the possessor to identify the seller or transferor -- within thirty days for citizens, five months for dealings with foreigners. All buying and selling must occur at a fixed location in the agora, with immediate payment and no credit.
summary: Freedmen face a twenty-year residency limit and foreigners a wealth ceiling, with death as the penalty for violations. Property claims require identifying the chain of transfer within set time limits. All market transactions must occur at designated agora locations with no credit allowed.
distilled: Freedmen and foreigners face strict residency and wealth limits enforced by death, while all commercial exchanges must occur at fixed agora locations without credit.
notes: The Athenian Stranger continues with laws on residency, property claims, and market regulation. -- The twenty-year residency limit for freedmen is without exact parallel in Greek law but reflects the Laws' broader anxiety about non-citizen accumulation of wealth and influence. -- The wealth ceiling tied to the third class census connects to the four-class property system established in Book V (744b-745b). -- The death penalty for exceeding wealth limits is extreme even by ancient standards, reflecting Plato's conviction that unchecked foreign wealth threatens civic stability. -- The requirement that all transactions occur at a fixed agora location with no credit is a radical anti-market measure designed to prevent fraud and speculation. Greek agoras typically had designated areas for different goods. -- The five-month window for foreign transactions, centered on the summer solstice, reflects the seasonal nature of ancient Mediterranean trade, which depended on favorable sailing conditions between spring and autumn.

narrative:
And if in any other manner or in any other place there be an exchange of one thing for another, and the seller give credit to the man who buys from him, he must do this on the understanding that the law gives no protection in cases of things sold not in accordance with these regulations. Again, as to contributions, any man who likes may go about collecting contributions as a friend among friends, but if any difference arises about the collection, he is to act on the understanding that the law gives no protection in such cases.

He who sells anything above the value of fifty drachmas shall be required to remain in the city for ten days, and the purchaser shall be informed of the house of the seller, with a view to the sort of charges which are apt to arise in such cases, and the restitutions which the law allows.

And let legal restitution be on this wise: If a man sells a slave who is in a consumption, or who has the disease of the stone, or of strangury, or epilepsy, or some other tedious and incurable disorder of body or mind, which is not discernible to the ordinary man, if the purchaser be a physician or trainer, he shall have no right of restitution; nor shall there be any right of restitution if the seller has told the truth beforehand to the buyer.
refashioned:
If an exchange occurs anywhere other than the designated location, or if the seller extends credit to the buyer, the parties must understand that the law provides no protection for transactions conducted outside these regulations. The same applies to friendly collections: anyone may go around gathering contributions among friends, but if a dispute arises over such collections, the law offers no recourse.

Anyone who sells goods worth more than fifty drachmas must remain in the city for ten days after the sale. The buyer must be told where the seller lives, so that any charges or claims for restitution that typically arise in such cases can be properly pursued.

The rules for legal restitution are as follows. If someone sells a slave suffering from consumption, kidney stones, urinary disease, epilepsy, or any other chronic and incurable disorder of body or mind that is not apparent to an ordinary person, and the buyer is a physician or a trainer, there shall be no right of restitution -- the buyer should have known better. Likewise, there shall be no right of restitution if the seller disclosed the condition truthfully before the sale.
elegant: Transactions outside the designated agora location and credit sales receive no legal protection. Friendly collections are similarly unprotected if disputed. Sellers of goods worth over fifty drachmas must remain in the city for ten days and disclose their address. In slave sales, there is no right of restitution if the buyer is a medical expert who should have detected the defect, or if the seller disclosed the condition honestly beforehand.
summary: The law denies protection to off-market transactions and credit sales. For sales over fifty drachmas, sellers must remain accessible for ten days. Slave sales allow restitution for undisclosed chronic illness, except when the buyer has medical expertise or the seller disclosed the defect.
distilled: Off-market and credit transactions receive no legal protection, while slave sales permit restitution for hidden defects unless the buyer had expert knowledge or the seller disclosed the condition.
notes: The Athenian Stranger addresses consumer protection in slave sales. -- The fifty-drachma threshold was a substantial sum; a skilled laborer in 4th-century Athens earned roughly one drachma per day. -- The requirement that sellers remain in the city for ten days is an early form of consumer protection, ensuring the buyer has recourse. -- The diseases listed (consumption/phthisis, kidney stones, strangury/urinary obstruction, epilepsy) were all well-known chronic conditions in Greek medicine; the Hippocratic corpus contains detailed discussions of each. -- The 'buyer beware' exception for physicians and trainers reflects Greek respect for specialized expertise -- a trained eye should catch what ordinary buyers cannot. -- The truth-disclosure defense anticipates modern doctrines of caveat emptor with disclosure requirements. -- Compare Republic II (382a-d) on the permissibility of deception, and Hippocratic writings on the ethical obligations of medical practitioners.

narrative:
But if a skilled person sells to another who is not skilled, let the buyer appeal for restitution within six months, except in the case of epilepsy, and then the appeal may be made within a year. The cause shall be determined by such physicians as the parties may agree to choose; and the defendant, if he lose the suit, shall pay double the price at which he sold.

If a private person sell to another private person, he shall have the right of restitution, and the decision shall be given as before, but the defendant, if he be cast, shall only pay back the price of the slave.

If a person sells a homicide to another, and they both know of the fact, let there be no restitution in such a case, but if he do not know of the fact, there shall be a right of restitution, whenever the buyer makes the discovery; and the decision shall rest with the five youngest guardians of the law, and if the decision be that the seller was cognisant the fact, he shall purify the house of the purchaser, according to the law of the interpreters, and shall pay back three times the purchase-money.
refashioned:
If a skilled person sells a defective slave to someone without expertise, the buyer may seek restitution within six months -- except in cases of epilepsy, where the appeal period extends to a full year. The case shall be judged by physicians agreed upon by both parties. If the seller loses, he must pay double the selling price.

If one ordinary person sells to another ordinary person, the same right of restitution applies and the same adjudication process is followed, but the losing seller need only refund the original price of the slave.

If someone sells a slave who has committed homicide, and both buyer and seller know this, there shall be no restitution. But if the buyer did not know, he has the right of restitution from the moment of discovery. The case shall be decided by the five youngest guardians of the law. If they determine the seller knew about the homicide, he must purify the buyer's house according to the rites prescribed by the religious interpreters, and pay back three times the purchase price.
elegant: When a skilled seller deceives an unskilled buyer about a slave's condition, the buyer has six months for restitution (one year for epilepsy), judged by agreed-upon physicians, with double the price as penalty. Between ordinary persons, only the original price is refunded. If a slave has committed homicide and the seller concealed this, he must purify the buyer's house and pay triple the price.
summary: Restitution rules for slave sales vary by the parties' expertise: skilled sellers who deceive unskilled buyers pay double, ordinary sellers refund the original price. Concealing a slave's homicide requires triple repayment plus ritual purification of the buyer's household.
distilled: The penalty for selling defective slaves scales with the seller's expertise and the severity of concealment -- double for expert deception, triple for hiding a homicide.
notes: The Athenian Stranger continues the consumer protection provisions for slave sales. -- The extended one-year period for epilepsy claims reflects the condition's episodic nature; seizures might not manifest for months. Greek medical writers recognized this diagnostic difficulty. -- The use of physician-arbitrators is an early form of expert witness testimony in legal proceedings. -- The graduated penalty structure (single, double, triple restitution) shows sophisticated legal reasoning about proportional justice. -- The homicide provision addresses ritual pollution (miasma): a slave who has killed someone carries religious contamination that can defile the buyer's household. -- The 'interpreters' (exegetai) were official religious authorities in Athens who advised on matters of purification and religious law. -- The five youngest guardians of the law handle this case, perhaps because it requires energy and investigation rather than the accumulated wisdom expected of elders. -- Compare Laws IX (865a-874d) on homicide law and purification requirements.

narrative:
If man exchanges either money for money, or anything whatever for anything else, either with or without life, let him give and receive them genuine and unadulterated, in accordance with the law.

And let us have a prelude about all this sort of roguery, like the preludes of our other laws. Every man should regard adulteration as of one and the same class with falsehood and deceit, concerning which the many are too fond of saying that at proper times and places the practice may often be right. But they leave the occasion, and the when, and the where, undefined and unsettled, and from this want of definiteness in their language they do a great deal of harm to themselves and to others.

Now a legislator ought not to leave the matter undetermined; he ought to prescribe some limit, either greater or less. Let this be the rule prescribed: No one shall call the Gods to witness, when he says or does anything false or deceitful or dishonest, unless he would be the most hateful of mankind to them. And he is most hateful to them takes a false oath, and pays no heed to the Gods; and in the next degree, he who tells a falsehood in the presence of his superiors. Now better men are the superiors of worse men, and in general elders are the superiors of the young; wherefore also parents are the superiors of their offspring, and men of women and children, and rulers of their subjects; for all men ought to reverence any one who is in any position of authority, and especially those who are in state offices.

And this is the reason why I have spoken of these matters. For every one who is guilty of adulteration in the agora tells a falsehood, and deceives, and when he invokes the Gods, according to the customs and cautions of the wardens of the agora, he does but swear without any respect for God or man. Certainly, it is an excellent rule not lightly to defile the names of the Gods, after the fashion of men in general, who care little about piety and purity in their religious actions.
refashioned:
Whenever anyone exchanges money for money, or anything for anything else -- whether living or not -- let everything given and received be genuine and unadulterated, in accordance with the law.

Let us offer a prelude on this kind of fraud, as we have done with our other laws. Everyone should understand that adulteration belongs to the same class as falsehood and deceit. People are far too fond of saying that deception can be perfectly appropriate at the right time and place. But they never define exactly when and where, and this vagueness causes enormous harm to themselves and others.

A legislator must not leave such matters undefined. He must draw a line somewhere. Here is the rule: no one shall invoke the gods to back up anything false, deceitful, or dishonest, unless he wishes to become the most despised of all people in the eyes of the gods. The person most hateful to the gods is he who swears a false oath with no regard for them. Next most hateful is he who tells lies in the presence of his superiors. Better people are the superiors of worse people; generally, elders are the superiors of the young -- and therefore parents of their children, men of women and children, and rulers of their subjects. Everyone ought to show reverence to those in positions of authority, and especially to holders of state office.

This is precisely why I have raised these points. Every person who adulterates goods in the marketplace is telling a falsehood and committing fraud. When such a person invokes the gods -- following the marketplace customs overseen by the agora wardens -- he swears falsely with no genuine respect for gods or men. It is an excellent principle never to defile the names of the gods carelessly, in the manner of those who show little concern for piety and purity in their religious observances.
elegant: All goods exchanged must be genuine and unadulterated. Adulteration is a form of falsehood and deceit, and the popular excuse that deception is acceptable at the right time and place is dangerously vague. The legislator must draw clear lines. The rule: no one shall invoke the gods in support of fraud. The most hateful person to the gods is the false oath-taker; next is the liar who deceives his superiors -- elders, parents, rulers. Every marketplace cheat who swears by the gods while selling adulterated goods defiles their names. The names of the gods should never be invoked carelessly.
summary: All commercial goods must be genuine. Adulteration is classified alongside falsehood and deceit, and the vague excuse that deception is sometimes acceptable must be replaced by clear rules. False oaths in the marketplace are the worst form of impiety, defiling the names of the gods.
distilled: Adulteration of goods is falsehood and deceit; invoking the gods in support of commercial fraud is the worst form of impiety.
notes: The Athenian Stranger introduces the prelude on marketplace fraud. -- The 'prelude' (prooimion) is a distinctive feature of Plato's legislative method in the Laws: persuasive preambles that explain the rationale before each law, discussed at length in Book IV (722d-723d). -- The hierarchical chain of superiors (elders over young, parents over children, men over women, rulers over subjects) reflects conventional Greek social structure. -- The severity attached to false oaths connects to Greek religious practice, where oath-taking was fundamental to legal proceedings and commercial transactions. Perjury was considered an offense against the gods themselves. -- Compare Republic II (382a-383c), where Socrates argues that the gods never deceive and that falsehood is always hateful to them. -- The agora wardens (agoranomoi) were real Athenian officials responsible for market regulation, weights and measures, and preventing fraud. Aristotle describes their functions in the Constitution of Athens (51.1).

narrative:
But if a man will not conform to this rule, let the law be as follows: He who sells anything in the agora shall not ask two prices for that which he sells, but he shall ask one price, and if he do not obtain this, he shall take away his goods; and on that day he shall not value them either at more or less; and there shall be no praising of any goods, or oath taken about them.

If a person disobeys this command, any citizen who is present, not being less than thirty years of age, may with impunity chastise and beat the swearer, but if instead of obeying the laws he takes no heed, he shall be liable to the charge of having betrayed them.

If a man sells any adulterated goods and will not obey these regulations, he who knows and can prove the fact, and does prove it in the presence of the magistrates, if he be a slave or a metic, shall have the adulterated goods; but if he be a citizen, and do not pursue the charge, he shall be called a rogue, and deemed to have robbed the Gods of the agora; or if he proves the charge, he shall dedicate the goods to the Gods of the agora.

He who is proved to have sold any adulterated goods, in addition to losing the goods themselves, shall be beaten with stripes -- a stripe for a drachma, according to the price of the goods; and the herald shall proclaim in the agora the offence for which he is going to be beaten.
refashioned:
For those who will not follow this principle, here is the law. Anyone selling in the agora must name a single price for his goods. If the buyer will not pay it, the seller shall take his goods away. On that same day, he shall not raise or lower the price. There shall be no extravagant praising of goods and no oaths sworn about them.

If anyone disobeys, any citizen present who is at least thirty years old may freely punish and beat the oath-taker. But if that citizen sees the violation and does nothing, he himself becomes liable for betraying the laws.

If someone sells adulterated goods and refuses to comply with these regulations, whoever can detect and prove the fraud before the magistrates shall receive the adulterated goods as a reward -- provided he is a slave or resident alien. If the person who knows about the fraud is a citizen and fails to bring the charge, he shall be called a rogue and considered a thief from the gods of the marketplace. But if the citizen does prove the charge, he shall dedicate the goods to the gods of the agora.

The person convicted of selling adulterated goods shall lose the goods themselves and also be beaten publicly -- one lash for each drachma of the goods' value. A herald shall announce in the marketplace the offense for which the beating is administered.
elegant: Sellers must name one fixed price with no haggling, no extravagant praise, and no oaths. Citizens over thirty may beat oath-breakers on the spot, and those who witness violations but do nothing betray the laws. Slaves or metics who prove adulteration receive the fraudulent goods; citizens who prove it must dedicate them to the gods of the agora. Convicted adulterators lose their goods and receive one lash per drachma of the goods' value, publicly announced by a herald.
summary: The marketplace law requires fixed single pricing with no oaths or haggling. Citizens must enforce the rules or be guilty of betraying the laws. Convicted adulterators lose their goods and are publicly beaten -- one lash per drachma of value.
distilled: Sellers must set one honest price with no oaths; adulterators are publicly beaten at one lash per drachma and lose their goods.
notes: The Athenian Stranger prescribes specific marketplace regulations. -- The single-price rule eliminates bargaining and what modern economists call price discrimination. This reflects Plato's deep suspicion of commercial cleverness. -- The provision empowering citizens to beat oath-breakers and holding passive citizens liable is characteristic of the Laws' vision of active citizen enforcement of morality. -- The different treatment of citizen vs. non-citizen informers reflects the Laws' status hierarchy: citizens must dedicate seized goods to the gods because they should not profit materially from market policing, while slaves and metics may keep the goods as an incentive. -- The public beating and herald's proclamation serve both punitive and deterrent functions -- public shaming was central to Greek conceptions of punishment. -- One lash per drachma creates a proportional penalty tied to the scale of the fraud. -- The 'Gods of the agora' likely include Hermes (god of commerce and thieves) and Zeus Agoraios (Zeus of the marketplace), who were commonly worshipped in Greek market squares.

narrative:
The warden of the agora and the guardians of the law shall obtain information from experienced persons about the rogueries and adulterations of the sellers, and shall write up what the seller ought and ought not to do in each case; and let them inscribe their laws on a column in front of the court of the wardens of the agora, that they may be clear instructors of those who have business in the agora.

Enough has been said in what has preceded about the wardens of the city, and if anything seems to be wanting, let them communicate with the guardians of the law, and write down the omission, and place on a column in the court of the wardens of the city the primary and secondary regulations which are laid down for them about their office.
refashioned:
The warden of the agora and the guardians of the law shall consult experienced persons to learn about the various frauds and adulterations practiced by sellers. They shall then write out clear rules specifying what sellers must and must not do in each type of case. These regulations shall be inscribed on a column in front of the agora wardens' court, so they serve as visible guidelines for everyone doing business in the marketplace.

Enough has already been said about the wardens of the city. If any gaps in their regulations become apparent, they shall consult with the guardians of the law, record whatever is missing, and inscribe both the primary and supplementary regulations for their office on a column at the city wardens' court.
elegant: The agora wardens and guardians of the law shall consult experienced persons about marketplace fraud, draft clear rules for sellers, and inscribe them on a public column at the agora court. Similarly, the city wardens shall fill any gaps in their regulations by consulting the guardians of the law and publicly inscribing all rules at their own court.
summary: Market regulations must be developed through expert consultation, clearly written out, and publicly inscribed on columns at the relevant courts, ensuring transparent and accessible rules for all who do business.
distilled: Marketplace rules against fraud must be developed with expert input and inscribed on public columns for all to see.
notes: The Athenian Stranger concludes the marketplace regulations with administrative provisions. -- The practice of inscribing laws on public columns (stelai) was standard in Greek cities; many surviving inscriptions of Athenian laws and decrees were carved on stone stelai and erected in public spaces. -- The requirement to consult 'experienced persons' about trade fraud reflects a practical, empirical approach to regulation -- lawmakers should learn from those with actual market knowledge. -- This passage bridges two administrative offices: the wardens of the agora (agoranomoi) and the wardens of the city (astynomoi). Both are described in detail in Laws VI (763d-764c). -- The emphasis on publicly accessible written law connects to the broader Greek democratic principle that laws must be visible and knowable to all citizens, a principle championed by Solon's reforms in early 6th-century Athens.

narrative:
After the practices of adulteration naturally follow the practices of retail trade. Concerning these, we will first of all give a word of counsel and reason, and the law shall come afterwards. Retail trade in a city is not by nature intended to do any harm, but quite the contrary; for is not he a benefactor who reduces the inequalities and incommensurabilities of goods to equality and common measure? And this is what the power of money accomplishes, and the merchant may be said to be appointed for this purpose. The hireling and the tavern-keeper, and many other occupations, some of them more and others less seemly -- alike have this object; they seek to satisfy our needs and equalize our possessions.

Let us then endeavour to see what has brought retail trade into ill-odour, and wherein lies the dishonour and unseemliness of it, in order that if not entirely, we may yet partially, cure the evil by legislation. To effect this is no easy matter, and requires a great deal of virtue.

Cleinias: "What do you mean?"
refashioned:
The topic of adulteration leads naturally to the broader subject of retail trade. Let us begin with some words of counsel and reason before we turn to the law itself.

Retail trade in a city is not inherently harmful -- quite the opposite. Is not the person who reduces the inequalities and incommensurabilities among goods to equality and common measure a genuine benefactor? This is exactly what money accomplishes, and the merchant can be said to exist for this purpose. Hired laborers, innkeepers, and many other occupations -- some more respectable than others -- all serve the same function: satisfying needs and equalizing possessions.

So let us try to understand what has given retail trade its bad reputation, and where exactly the dishonor and unseemliness lie, so that we can cure the problem through legislation -- if not completely, then at least partially. This is no easy task, and it requires a great deal of virtue.

"What do you mean?" asked Cleinias.
elegant: Retail trade is not inherently harmful but actually beneficial: merchants perform the vital function of reducing inequalities among goods to a common measure through money. Laborers, innkeepers, and other tradespeople all serve the same purpose of satisfying needs and equalizing possessions. The challenge is to understand why trade has acquired a bad reputation, so that legislation can cure the evil -- no easy task.
summary: The Athenian Stranger argues that retail trade is inherently beneficial, since merchants equalize goods through the medium of money. The problem lies not in trade itself but in the corruption that accompanies it, which legislation must address.
distilled: Retail trade is inherently beneficial because it equalizes goods through money, but its bad reputation stems from the moral corruption of those who practice it.
notes: The Athenian Stranger speaks, with Cleinias responding. -- This passage offers one of the most positive assessments of commerce in ancient philosophy, recognizing the merchant's social function as an equalizer of goods. -- The concept of money as a 'common measure' (koinon metron) anticipates Aristotle's more developed treatment in Nicomachean Ethics V.5 (1133a-b), where money is described as making goods commensurable. -- The distinction between the nature of trade (beneficial) and its practice (corrupted) is philosophically significant and contrasts with the more straightforwardly negative view of commerce found in Republic II (371c-372a). -- The phrase 'incommensurabilities of goods' uses mathematical language, reflecting Plato's interest in proportion and measure as principles of good order -- themes central to the Philebus and Statesman. -- Plato's acknowledgment that curing commercial corruption 'requires a great deal of virtue' is remarkably realistic for a philosopher often accused of utopianism.

narrative:
"Dear Cleinias, the class of men is small -- they must have been rarely gifted by nature, and trained by education -- who, when assailed by wants and desires, are able to hold out and observe moderation, and when they might make a great deal of money are sober in their wishes, and prefer a moderate to a large gain. But the mass of mankind are the very opposite: their desires are unbounded, and when they might gain in moderation they prefer gains without limit; wherefore all that relates to retail trade, and merchandise, and the keeping of taverns, is denounced and numbered among dishonourable things.

"For if what I trust may never be and will not be, we were to compel, if I may venture to say a ridiculous thing, the best men everywhere to keep taverns for a time, or carry on retail trade, or do anything of that sort; or if, in consequence of some fate or necessity, the best women were compelled to follow similar callings, then we should know how agreeable and pleasant all these things are; and if all such occupations were managed on incorrupt principles, they would be honoured as we honour a mother or a nurse."
refashioned:
"My dear Cleinias, only a rare few -- people who must be exceptionally gifted by nature and well-trained by education -- can resist the pull of wants and desires and maintain moderation. When they could make a fortune, they remain measured in their ambitions, preferring modest gain to excessive profit. But the great majority of people are the exact opposite: their appetites are limitless, and when moderate gain is available they chase unlimited profit instead. This is why everything connected with retail trade, merchandising, and tavern-keeping is condemned and counted among dishonorable occupations.

"Here is a ridiculous thought experiment, if you will indulge me -- something I hope will never actually happen. Suppose we compelled the best men everywhere to run taverns or conduct retail business for a while, or if by some twist of fate the best women were forced into similar roles. Then we would discover how pleasant and agreeable all these activities truly are. If such occupations were conducted on honest principles, they would be honored the way we honor a mother or a nurse."
elegant: Only a rare few can resist the temptation of unlimited gain. Most people's desires are boundless, which is why commerce and tavern-keeping are condemned. Yet if the most virtuous people were compelled to practice these trades, we would see how agreeable they can be. Conducted on honest principles, commerce would be honored like motherhood and nursing.
summary: The Athenian Stranger explains that trade is dishonorable not by nature but because most people lack the moderation to practice it virtuously. If the best people ran shops and taverns, we would see that commerce conducted honestly deserves the same respect as motherhood.
distilled: Commerce is dishonored not because of its nature but because most people lack the self-control to practice it with moderation.
notes: The Athenian Stranger speaks to Cleinias. -- The thought experiment of compelling virtuous people to practice trade is one of Plato's most striking rhetorical devices in the Laws, using absurdity to reveal a philosophical point. -- The comparison of honest commerce to motherhood and nursing is remarkable for its elevation of caring labor. -- The analysis of insatiable desire (pleonexia) as the root corruption of commerce connects to Republic II (359c), where Glaucon describes the natural human tendency toward 'having more.' -- The distinction between the rare moderate person and the greedy majority echoes Republic IX (580d-583a), where Socrates describes the philosophic soul as the only one capable of evaluating pleasures correctly. -- Aristotle later develops the distinction between natural and unnatural wealth-getting in Politics I.8-10, drawing heavily on this passage from the Laws.

narrative:
"But now that a man goes to desert places and builds houses which can only be reached by long journeys, for the sake of retail trade, and receives strangers who are in need at the welcome resting-place, and gives them peace and calm when they are tossed by the storm, or cool shade in the heat; and then instead of behaving to them as friends, and showing the duties of hospitality to his guests, treats them as enemies and captives who are at his mercy, and will not release them until they have paid the most unjust, abominable, and extortionate ransom -- these are the sort of practices, and foul evils they are, which cast a reproach upon the succour of adversity.

"And the legislator ought always to be devising a remedy for evils of this nature. There is an ancient saying, which is also a true one -- 'To fight against two opponents is a difficult thing,' as is seen in diseases and in many other cases. And in this case also the war is against two enemies -- wealth and poverty; one of whom corrupts the soul of man with luxury, while the other drives him by pain into utter shamelessness.

"What remedy can a city of sense find against this disease? In the first place, they must have as few retail traders as possible; and in the second place, they must assign the occupation to that class of men whose corruption will be the least injury to the state; and in the third place, they must devise some way whereby the followers of these occupations themselves will not readily fall into habits of unbridled shamelessness and meanness."
refashioned:
"But consider what actually happens. A man sets up an inn in a remote area, accessible only by long journeys, for the sake of retail profit. He welcomes weary travelers who need shelter, offering them peace and calm when they are storm-tossed, or cool shade in the heat. But then, instead of treating them as friends and showing the hospitality that guests deserve, he treats them as enemies and prisoners at his mercy, refusing to release them until they have paid the most outrageous and extortionate ransom. These are the foul practices that cast a shadow over what should be a service to people in need.

"The legislator must constantly seek remedies for evils like these. As the ancient and true saying goes, 'Fighting on two fronts is difficult' -- and this applies to diseases and many other situations. Here the battle is against two enemies: wealth and poverty. Wealth corrupts the soul with luxury, while poverty drives people through pain into utter shamelessness.

"What remedy can a sensible city find for this disease? First, keep the number of retail traders as small as possible. Second, assign the occupation to the class of people whose corruption would cause the least harm to the state. Third, devise some system to prevent those who follow these occupations from easily falling into habits of shameless greed and meanness."
elegant: Innkeepers who exploit vulnerable travelers -- offering shelter only to extort outrageous prices -- represent the worst corruption of commerce. The legislator faces a war on two fronts: wealth corrupts through luxury, poverty through shamelessness. The remedy has three parts: minimize the number of traders, assign the occupation to those whose corruption would least harm the state, and create safeguards against the traders themselves falling into shameless greed.
summary: The Athenian Stranger describes how innkeepers exploit stranded travelers and identifies the twin enemies of commercial virtue: wealth (which corrupts through luxury) and poverty (which drives people to shamelessness). The three-part remedy is to minimize traders, assign the role to those least harmful if corrupted, and create safeguards against moral decline.
distilled: Commerce is corrupted by two enemies -- wealth breeding luxury and poverty breeding shamelessness -- and the remedy is to minimize traders, assign the role carefully, and safeguard against moral decline.
notes: The Athenian Stranger continues his analysis of commercial corruption. -- The vivid description of predatory innkeepers exploiting travelers reflects real conditions on ancient Greek roads, where hospitality (xenia) was both a sacred obligation and a practical necessity. -- The proverb 'to fight on two fronts is difficult' (chalepou pros duo) is attributed to various sources; it appears in a similar context in Aristophanes. -- The twin-enemy analysis (wealth vs. poverty) directly echoes Republic IV (421d-422a), where Socrates warns that both wealth and poverty corrupt the craftsman -- wealth making him idle, poverty making his work inferior. -- The three-part remedy (minimize, assign carefully, safeguard) represents practical political wisdom rather than utopian idealism, showing the mature Plato's accommodation to human nature. -- The metaphor of commerce as a 'disease' requiring a 'remedy' reflects the medical analogies pervasive in the Laws, where legislation is repeatedly compared to medicine for the soul.

narrative: "After this preface let our law run as follows, and may fortune favour us: No landowner among the Magnetes, whose city the God is restoring and resettling -- no one, that is, of the 5040 families -- shall become a retail trader either voluntarily or involuntarily; neither shall he be a merchant, or do any service for private persons unless they equally serve him, except for his father or his mother, and their fathers and mothers; and in general for his elders who are freemen, and whom he serves as a freeman."
refashioned: "After this preface, let our law be stated as follows -- and may fortune favor us. No landowner among the Magnetes, whose city the god is restoring and resettling -- that is, no one belonging to the 5,040 citizen families -- shall engage in retail trade, whether voluntarily or involuntarily. Nor shall any citizen be a merchant, or perform services for private individuals unless those individuals equally serve him in return. The only exceptions are service to his father and mother, their parents, and in general to his freeborn elders, whom he serves as a free man serves."
elegant: The law: no citizen of the 5,040 families of Magnesia shall engage in retail trade, whether voluntarily or by compulsion, nor act as a merchant, nor serve private individuals unless the service is mutual. The only exceptions are service to parents, grandparents, and freeborn elders.
summary: The law prohibits all citizens of Magnesia's 5,040 families from engaging in retail trade or one-sided service to private individuals, with exceptions only for service to parents, grandparents, and freeborn elders.
distilled: No citizen of Magnesia's 5,040 founding families shall engage in retail trade or unreciprocated private service, except to family elders.
notes: The Athenian Stranger states the law on citizen commerce. -- The 5,040 households is the fixed number of citizen families established for Magnesia in Laws V (737e-738a), chosen for its mathematical properties (it is divisible by all numbers from 1 to 12 except 11). -- The complete prohibition of citizen involvement in retail trade is more radical than Athenian practice, where citizens could engage in commerce though it carried social stigma. Sparta, by contrast, did prohibit its citizens from trade, a policy the Laws partly follows. -- The exception for service to parents and elders reflects the deep Greek obligation of filial piety, which Book 11 will treat at length in passages 35-39. -- The phrase 'whose city the God is restoring' refers to the mythological framing of Magnesia's founding under divine guidance, discussed in Laws III-IV. -- Compare Aristotle, Politics VII.9 (1328b-1329a), where citizens of the ideal state are similarly excluded from commercial and mechanical occupations.

narrative:
"Now it is difficult to determine accurately the things which are worthy or unworthy of a freeman, but let those who have obtained the prize of virtue give judgment about them in accordance with their feelings of right and wrong. He who in any way shares in the illiberality of retail trades may be indicted for dishonouring his race by any one who likes, before those who have been judged to be the first in virtue; and if he appear to throw dirt upon his father's house by an unworthy occupation, let him be imprisoned for a year and abstain from that sort of thing; and if he repeat the offence, for two years; and every time that he is convicted let the length of his imprisonment be doubled.

"This shall be the second law: He who engages in retail trade must be either a metic or a stranger.

"And a third law shall be: In order that the retail trader who dwells in our city may be as good or as little bad as possible, the guardians of the law shall remember that they are not only guardians of those who may be easily watched and prevented from becoming lawless or bad, because they are wellborn and bred; but still more should they have a watch over those who are of another sort, and follow pursuits which have a very strong tendency to make men bad. And, therefore, in respect of the multifarious occupations of retail trade -- that is to say, in respect of such of them as are allowed to remain, because they seem to be quite necessary in a state -- about these the guardians of the law should meet and take counsel with those who have experience of the several kinds of retail trade, as we before commanded, concerning adulteration (which is a matter akin to this), and when they meet they shall consider what amount of receipts, after deducting expenses, will produce a moderate gain to the retail trades, and they shall fix in writing and strictly maintain what they find to be the right percentage of profit; this shall be seen to by the wardens of the agora, and by the wardens of the city, and by the wardens of the country. And so retail trade will benefit every one, and do the least possible injury to those in the state who practise it."
refashioned:
"It is difficult to determine precisely which activities are worthy or unworthy of a free person, so let those who have been recognized for their virtue make these judgments according to their sense of right and wrong. Any citizen who participates in the pettiness of retail trade may be indicted for dishonoring his lineage by anyone who wishes, before a panel of those judged first in virtue. If he is found to have disgraced his family's name through an unworthy occupation, he shall be imprisoned for one year and required to cease such activity. For a second offense, two years. Each subsequent conviction doubles the sentence.

"The second law: only resident aliens or foreigners may engage in retail trade.

"The third law: to ensure that the traders living in our city are as good -- or as little bad -- as possible, the guardians of the law must remember that their duty extends beyond watching over the wellborn, who are easily kept from lawlessness. They must watch even more carefully over those of different backgrounds who follow occupations with a strong tendency to corrupt character. Therefore, regarding the various forms of retail trade that must be allowed because they are essential to the state, the guardians of the law shall consult with experienced practitioners of each type of trade -- as we previously directed regarding adulteration. Together they shall determine what level of revenue, after deducting expenses, will yield a moderate profit for the trader, and they shall fix in writing and strictly enforce what they find to be the right profit margin. The wardens of the agora, the wardens of the city, and the wardens of the country shall oversee enforcement. In this way, retail trade will benefit everyone while causing the least possible harm to those who practice it."
elegant: It is difficult to determine precisely what activities are worthy of a free person, so those recognized for virtue shall judge. Citizens who engage in retail trade may be indicted for dishonoring their lineage, with escalating imprisonment for repeat offenses -- one year, then two, then doubling each time. Only resident aliens and foreigners may legally be traders. To keep traders as virtuous as possible, the guardians of the law shall consult experienced merchants about each kind of trade, determine what level of revenue after expenses produces a moderate profit, and fix the right percentage in writing. The wardens of agora, city, and country shall strictly enforce these margins. In this way commerce benefits everyone while causing the least possible harm to those who practice it.
summary: Three laws govern retail trade: citizens who engage in it face escalating imprisonment; only non-citizens may practice it; and the guardians of the law must set and enforce fair profit margins in consultation with experienced traders, ensuring commerce benefits all while minimizing moral harm.
distilled: Only non-citizens may engage in retail trade, with fair profit margins set and enforced by the guardians of the law in consultation with experienced practitioners.
notes: The Athenian Stranger lays out three interrelated commercial laws. -- The escalating imprisonment (1, 2, 4, 8 years, etc.) for citizens engaging in trade is exceptionally harsh, reflecting the seriousness with which Plato treats the corruption of citizen virtue. -- The restriction of trade to metics and foreigners mirrors Spartan practice and contrasts with Athens, where citizen participation in commerce was unrestricted though socially stigmatized. -- The provision for regulated profit margins is remarkably modern, resembling medieval just-price theory later developed by Thomas Aquinas (Summa Theologiae II-II, Q.77). -- The requirement to consult experienced traders before setting regulations shows Plato's pragmatic recognition that lawmakers need specialized knowledge, consistent with the expert-consultation principle in passage 11.10. -- The concept of 'moderate gain' (metrios kerdos) connects to the broader theme of moderation (sophrosyne) that pervades the Laws. -- Compare Aristotle, Politics I.9-10, where he distinguishes natural household management (oikonomike) from unnatural unlimited acquisition (chrematistike).

narrative:
"When a man makes an agreement which he does not fulfil, unless the agreement be of a nature which the law or a vote of the assembly does not allow, or which he has made under the influence of some unjust compulsion, or which he is prevented from fulfilling against his will by some unexpected chance, the other party may go to law with him in the courts of the tribes, for not having completed his agreement, if the parties are not able previously to come to terms before arbiters or before their neighbours.

"The class of craftsmen who have furnished human life with the arts is dedicated to Hephaestus and Athene; and there is a class of craftsmen who preserve the works of all craftsmen by arts of defence, the votaries of Ares and Athene, to which divinities they too are rightly dedicated. All these continue through life serving the country and the people; some of them are leaders in battle; others make for hire implements and works, and they ought not to deceive in such matters, out of respect to the Gods who are their ancestors.

"If any craftsman through indolence omit to execute his work in a given time, not reverencing the God who gives him the means of life, but considering, foolish fellow, that he is his own God and will let him off easily, in the first place, he shall suffer at the hands of the God, and in the second place, the law shall follow in a similar spirit. He shall owe to him who contracted with him the price of the works which he has failed in performing, and he shall begin again and execute them gratis in the given time.

"When a man undertakes a work, the law gives him the same advice which was given to the seller, that he should not attempt to raise the price, but simply ask the value; this the law enjoins also on the contractor; for the craftsman assuredly knows the value of his work."
refashioned:
"When a person makes an agreement and fails to fulfill it, the other party may sue in the tribal courts -- unless the agreement was prohibited by law or assembly vote, was made under unjust compulsion, or was prevented by unforeseen circumstances beyond the person's control. However, the parties should first attempt to resolve the matter through arbitration or mediation by their neighbors.

"The class of craftsmen who have equipped human life with the arts is dedicated to Hephaestus and Athena. There is also a class of craftsmen who protect the works of all other craftsmen through the arts of defense -- the devotees of Ares and Athena, to whom they are rightly dedicated. All of these serve the country and the people throughout their lives: some as leaders in battle, others making implements and works for hire. They must not deceive in such matters, out of respect for the gods who are their patrons.

"If a craftsman through laziness fails to complete his work on time -- not honoring the god who provides his livelihood, but foolishly thinking he is his own god who will let himself off easily -- he shall first suffer divine consequences, and then the law shall follow in the same spirit. He must refund the contracted price for the work he failed to perform, and he must start over and complete the work for free within the original timeframe.

"When someone undertakes a commission, the law gives the same advice as for sellers: do not try to inflate the price, but charge the fair value. The craftsman, after all, surely knows what his work is worth."
elegant: Broken agreements may be brought to tribal courts, unless the agreement was illegal, coerced, or prevented by unforeseeable events. Parties should first attempt resolution through arbiters or neighbors. The class of craftsmen who furnish human life with the arts is dedicated to Hephaestus and Athena; the class that defends all other crafts through the arts of war is dedicated to Ares and Athena. Neither class should deceive, out of respect for the gods who are their patrons. A lazy craftsman who misses his deadline, foolishly thinking he is his own god who will let himself off easily, must refund the contract price and redo the work for free within the original timeframe. Like sellers, contractors must charge fair value rather than inflated prices, for the craftsman surely knows what his work is worth.
summary: Contract law provides for enforcement of agreements in tribal courts, with exceptions for illegal, coerced, or force majeure situations. Craftsmen, as servants of the gods, must not deceive. Those who miss deadlines through laziness must refund their fee and redo the work without charge, and all must price their services at fair value.
distilled: Broken contracts are enforceable in court unless illegal, coerced, or prevented by chance; lazy craftsmen must refund their fee and complete the work for free.
notes: The Athenian Stranger addresses contract law and the obligations of craftsmen. -- The three exceptions to contractual enforcement (illegal agreements, duress, and force majeure) closely parallel modern contract defenses and reflect sophisticated legal reasoning. -- The requirement for prior arbitration or mediation before litigation is a feature of actual Athenian legal procedure; public arbitrators (diaitetai) were appointed to hear civil cases before they went to trial. -- The dedication of craftsmen to Hephaestus (god of the forge and metalworking) and Athena (goddess of wisdom and crafts) reflects genuine Greek religious practice; Athens had a major festival, the Hephaestia, honoring artisan culture. -- The striking phrase 'considering, foolish fellow, that he is his own God' recalls Republic II (381c-382a) on human presumption regarding divine matters. -- The penalty of free re-performance plus refund is more severe than simple contract damages, serving a punitive and moralizing function. -- The principle that a craftsman 'knows the value of his work' echoes Socrates' frequent appeals to craft knowledge as a model for ethical expertise.

narrative:
"Wherefore, in free states the man of art ought not to attempt to impose upon private individuals by the help of his art, which is by nature a true thing; and he who is wronged in a matter of this sort, shall have a right of action against the party who has wronged him.

"And if any one lets out work to a craftsman, and does not pay him duly according to the lawful agreement, disregarding Zeus the guardian of the city and Athene, who are the partners of the state, and overthrows the foundations of society for the sake of a little gain, in his case let the law and the Gods maintain the common bonds of the state. And let him who, having already received the work in exchange, does not pay the price in the time agreed, pay double the price; and if a year has elapsed, although interest is not to be taken on loans, yet for every drachma which he owes to the contractor let him pay a monthly interest of an obol.

"Suits about these matters are to be decided by the courts of the tribes; and by the way, since we have mentioned craftsmen at all, we must not forget the other craft of war, in which generals and tacticians are the craftsmen, who undertake voluntarily the work of our safety, as other craftsmen undertake other public works; if they execute their work well the law will never tire of praising him who gives them those honours which are the just rewards of the soldier; but if any one, having already received the benefit of any noble service in war, does not make the due return of honour, the law will blame him.

"Let this then be the law, having an ingredient of praise, not compelling but advising the great body of the citizens to honour the brave men who are the saviours of the whole state, whether by their courage or by their military skill; they should honour them, I say, in the second place; for the first and highest tribute of respect is to be given to those who are able above other men to honour the words of good legislators."
refashioned:
"In a free state, a skilled professional must not exploit private individuals through his expertise, which is by its nature something honest and true. Anyone wronged in this way has the right to bring an action against the offender.

"Equally, anyone who commissions work from a craftsman and then fails to pay according to the lawful agreement -- disregarding Zeus the guardian of the city and Athena who are the state's partners, and undermining the foundations of society for the sake of a small saving -- shall face the enforcement of law and the displeasure of the gods. Whoever receives the completed work but does not pay within the agreed time must pay double the price. If a full year passes, then despite the general prohibition on interest for loans, he must pay a monthly surcharge of one obol for every drachma owed.

"These disputes shall be resolved in the tribal courts. And since we have raised the subject of craftsmen, we should not overlook the craft of war. Generals and tacticians are craftsmen too, who voluntarily undertake the work of keeping us safe, just as other craftsmen undertake public works. When they do their job well, the law will never tire of praising those who give them the honors soldiers justly deserve. But anyone who has benefited from noble military service and fails to return the appropriate honor will be condemned by the law.

"This law carries an element of praise rather than compulsion, advising the citizenry to honor the brave men who save the state through courage or military skill. They should be honored in the second place, however; the first and highest respect belongs to those who, more than all others, are able to honor the words of good legislators."
elegant: In a free state, skilled professionals must not exploit private individuals through their art, which by nature is an honest thing; anyone wronged has a right of action. Equally, clients who fail to pay craftsmen according to lawful agreements disregard Zeus the guardian of the city and Athena, partners of the state, undermining the foundations of society for petty gain. Those who receive completed work but do not pay on time owe double, and after a year, interest accrues at one obol per drachma monthly despite the general prohibition on loan interest. Military leaders are craftsmen of public safety who deserve honor when they execute their work well, and the law shall never tire of praising those who honor them. Yet the highest tribute belongs not to soldiers but to those who are able above all others to honor the words of good legislators.
summary: Craftsmen must not exploit clients, and clients must pay on time or face double penalties and interest. Military leaders deserve honor as craftsmen of public safety, but the highest respect is reserved for those who uphold and honor the words of good legislators.
distilled: Both craftsmen and their clients owe honest dealing, with the highest civic honor going not to soldiers but to those who best honor the laws.
notes: The Athenian Stranger addresses both craftsman fraud and client non-payment. -- The invocation of Zeus Polieus (guardian of the city) and Athena Polias (city-protector) as 'partners of the state' reflects the deep integration of religion and civic life in Greek political thought. -- The interest rate of one obol per drachma per month (approximately 16.7% annually) is notable because the Laws generally prohibits interest on loans; this exception for contract non-payment shows the severity of the offense. -- The comparison of generals to craftsmen is characteristically Platonic, echoing the Republic's treatment of the guardian class as practitioners of a specialized art (techne). -- The hierarchy placing law-honoring citizens above military heroes is a distinctly Platonic value judgment, reversing the typical Greek prioritization of martial valor. Compare Laws I (630d-631d), where courage is ranked below wisdom and justice. -- The phrase 'the words of good legislators' (logoi ton agathon nomotheton) implicitly includes the Laws itself.

narrative:
"The greater part of the dealings between man and man have been now regulated by us with the exception of those that relate to orphans and the supervision of orphans by their guardians.
