"Now consider: if a man has a father or mother, or their parents, living in his house and stricken with age, he should realize that no statue can be more powerful in granting his requests than these living elders sitting at his own hearth -- provided he knows how to serve them properly."

"And what do you call the true mode of service?" asked Cleinias.

"I will tell you, my friend, for such things are worth hearing."

"Go on," said Cleinias.
elegant: No god or sensible person would advise neglecting parents. We honor the gods in two ways: by worshipping them directly or through lifeless statues. But aged parents sitting at one's own hearth are more powerful than any statue -- living shrines whose prayers and blessings can be won through proper service.
summary: The Athenian Stranger introduces the topic of filial duty by comparing aged parents to divine images: no statue is more potent than a living parent sitting at the family hearth, provided one knows how to serve them properly.
distilled: Aged parents at the hearth are more powerful intercessors than any divine statue, if only one knows how to honor them properly.
notes: The Athenian Stranger speaks, with Cleinias responding. -- The comparison of aged parents to divine statues is one of the most striking images in the Laws, elevating filial piety to the level of religious worship. -- The two forms of divine worship (direct observation of celestial gods and image-worship of others) reflects actual Greek religious practice: the visible gods include the sun, moon, and stars (cf. Laws X, 886d-887c), while image-worship involved temple statues and household shrines. -- The concept of the hearth (hestia) as a sacred center of the household was fundamental to Greek religion; Hestia was the goddess of the hearth. -- This passage anticipates the Confucian emphasis on filial piety (xiao) as the foundation of social virtue, though developed independently. -- Compare Republic I (331a-b), where Cephalus speaks of the comfort his wealth gives him regarding debts to both gods and men. -- The Athenian Stranger's promise that 'such things are worth listening to' signals the importance of the passage that follows.

narrative:
"Oedipus, as tradition says, when dishonoured by his sons, invoked on them curses which every one declares to have been heard and ratified by the Gods, and Amyntor in his wrath invoked curses on his son Phoenix, and Theseus upon Hippolytus, and innumerable others have also called down wrath upon their children, whence it is clear that the Gods listen to the imprecations of parents; for the curses of parents are, as they ought to be, mighty against their children as no others are.

"And shall we suppose that the prayers of a father or mother who is specially dishonoured by his or her children, are heard by the Gods in accordance with nature; and that if a parent is honoured by them, and in the gladness of his heart earnestly entreats the Gods in his prayers to do them good, he is not equally heard, and that they do not minister to his request? If not, they would be very unjust ministers of good, and that we affirm to be contrary to their nature."

Cleinias: "Certainly."
refashioned:
"Oedipus, as tradition tells us, cursed his sons when they dishonored him, and everyone agrees the gods heard and fulfilled those curses. Amyntor in his rage cursed his son Phoenix, and Theseus cursed Hippolytus, and countless others have called down divine wrath upon their children. From all this it is clear that the gods listen to the prayers of parents -- for parental curses are, as they rightly should be, more powerful against children than any other kind.

"If the gods hear the curses of dishonored parents in accordance with nature, must we not also suppose that when a parent is honored by their children and joyfully prays to the gods for their welfare, those prayers are equally heard? If the gods responded to curses but ignored blessings, they would be unjust dispensers of good -- and that, we maintain, is contrary to their nature."

"Certainly," said Cleinias.
elegant: Mythology proves that gods hear parental curses: Oedipus cursed his sons, Amyntor cursed Phoenix, Theseus cursed Hippolytus. If the gods answer curses against dishonorable children, they must equally answer blessings for honorable ones -- otherwise they would be unjust, which is contrary to their nature.
summary: The examples of Oedipus, Amyntor, and Theseus demonstrate that the gods hear parental curses. By the same logic, the gods must also hear parental blessings for dutiful children, since failing to do so would make them unjust.
distilled: If the gods hear parental curses against disobedient children, justice requires they equally hear parental blessings for dutiful ones.
notes: The Athenian Stranger speaks, with Cleinias affirming. -- Three mythological examples illustrate parental curses: Oedipus cursed his sons Eteocles and Polynices (Aeschylus, Seven Against Thebes; Sophocles, Oedipus at Colonus); Amyntor cursed Phoenix for sleeping with his concubine (Homer, Iliad IX.447-461); Theseus cursed Hippolytus after Phaedra's false accusation (Euripides, Hippolytus). -- The argument from divine justice -- that gods cannot selectively hear curses while ignoring blessings -- is a compact theological argument drawing on the principle established in Laws X that the gods are just by nature. -- Compare Republic II (364b-365a), where Adeimantus describes popular beliefs about gods being swayed by prayers and sacrifices. Here in the Laws, Plato uses the same popular beliefs constructively rather than critically. -- The emotional power of parental blessings and curses was deeply felt in Greek culture, where the dying words of parents were considered especially potent.

narrative:
"May we not think, as I was saying just now, that we can possess no image which is more honoured by the Gods, than that of a father or grandfather, or of a mother stricken in years? Whom when a man honours, the heart of the God rejoices, and he is ready to answer their prayers.

"And, truly, the figure of an ancestor is a wonderful thing, far higher than that of a lifeless image. For the living, when they are honoured by us, join in our prayers, and when they are dishonoured, they utter imprecations against us; but lifeless objects do neither.

"And therefore, if a man makes a right use of his father and grandfather and other aged relations, he will have images which above all others will win him the favour of the Gods."

Cleinias: "Excellent."
refashioned:
"As I was saying, we can possess no image more honored by the gods than a father, a grandfather, or a mother in her old age. When a man honors them, the heart of the god rejoices and he is ready to answer their prayers.

"A living ancestor is truly a wonderful thing -- far superior to any lifeless statue. The living, when we honor them, join their prayers to ours; when we dishonor them, they utter curses against us. Lifeless objects do neither.

"Therefore, if a man treats his father, grandfather, and other aged relations properly, he will possess the most powerful intercessors imaginable -- living images that, above all others, will win him the favor of the gods."

"Excellent," said Cleinias.
elegant: No divine image is more honored by the gods than a living parent or grandparent. Living ancestors surpass lifeless statues because they actively respond: when honored, they join their prayers to ours; when dishonored, they curse us. Proper treatment of aged relatives provides the most powerful intercessors with the gods.
summary: Living parents and grandparents are superior to any divine statue because they actively pray for those who honor them and curse those who dishonor them. Proper care of aged relatives is the most effective form of divine intercession.
distilled: Living parents are superior to lifeless statues as divine intercessors because they actively pray for or against their children depending on how they are treated.
notes: The Athenian Stranger speaks, with Cleinias affirming. -- The argument reaches its conclusion: the living parent is a more efficacious religious object than any carved image because the parent actively participates in the prayer relationship. -- This is a remarkable theological-ethical argument that elevates domestic care of the elderly into the highest form of religious practice. -- The phrase 'the heart of the God rejoices' is unusual in Plato, who typically avoids attributing emotions to the divine. Here in the Laws, he employs popular religious language strategically. -- The comparison between living and lifeless images has philosophical implications for the theory of representation: a living exemplar is always superior to a lifeless copy, connecting to the Republic's critique of mimesis (Book X). -- The practical implication is powerful: the most effective form of prayer is not temple worship but filial devotion. -- Compare the Roman concept of the paterfamilias and the lares (household gods representing ancestors) as parallel cultural expressions of ancestral reverence.

narrative:
"Every man of any understanding fears and respects the prayers of parents, knowing well that many times and to many persons they have been accomplished. Now these things being thus ordered by nature, good men think it a blessing from heaven if their parents live to old age and reach the utmost limit of human life, or if taken away before their time they are deeply regretted by them; but to bad men parents are always a cause of terror.

"Wherefore let every man honour with every sort of lawful honour his own parents, agreeably to what has now been said. But if this prelude be an unmeaning sound in the ears of any one, let the law follow, which may be rightly imposed in these terms: If any one in this city be not sufficiently careful of his parents, and do not regard and gratify in every respect their wishes more than those of his sons and of his other offspring or of himself -- let him who experiences this sort of treatment either come himself, or send some one to inform the three eldest guardians of the law, and three of the women who have the care of marriages; and let them look to the matter and punish youthful evil-doers with stripes and bonds if they are under thirty years of age, that is to say, if they be men, or if they be women, let them undergo the same punishment up to forty years of age."
refashioned:
"Every person of understanding fears and respects the prayers of parents, knowing full well that such prayers have been fulfilled many times for many people. Given how nature has ordered these things, good people consider it a divine blessing when their parents reach extreme old age, and they mourn deeply if their parents die prematurely. But for bad people, parents are always a source of dread.

"Therefore let every person honor their parents with every lawful form of respect, in accordance with what has been said. But if this prelude falls on deaf ears, let the law follow, and it may rightly be stated as follows: if anyone in this city fails to care sufficiently for his parents, and does not prioritize their wishes above those of his children, his other descendants, or even himself -- the neglected parent may either appear in person or send someone to report the situation to the three eldest guardians of the law and three of the women who oversee marriages. They shall investigate and punish young offenders: men under thirty and women under forty shall be subject to flogging and imprisonment."
elegant: Sensible people fear parental prayers, knowing they are often fulfilled. Good people consider long-lived parents a divine blessing; bad people dread them. The law requires that parents' wishes be prioritized above those of children or oneself. Neglected parents may report to the three eldest guardians of the law and three marriage-overseeing women. Offenders under thirty (men) or forty (women) face flogging and imprisonment.
summary: The prelude concludes by noting that wise people fear and respect parental prayers. The law requires parents' wishes to be prioritized above all others; neglected parents may report their mistreatment, and young offenders face corporal punishment.
distilled: Parents' wishes must be honored above all others, and those who neglect their parents face flogging and imprisonment upon complaint to the guardians.
notes: The Athenian Stranger transitions from prelude to enforceable law. -- The requirement to honor parents' wishes above those of one's own children or oneself is extraordinarily demanding, reversing the typical modern hierarchy of obligations. -- The different age thresholds for men (under 30) and women (under 40) receiving corporal punishment may reflect Greek assumptions about the different rates at which men and women achieve full moral responsibility. -- The joint involvement of male guardians of the law and female marriage overseers continues the pattern of mixed-gender institutional oversight in family matters. -- The phrase 'if this prelude be an unmeaning sound' candidly acknowledges that moral persuasion sometimes fails, necessitating coercive law -- a key structural principle of the Laws' legislative method. -- Compare Exodus 20:12 ('Honor thy father and thy mother'), the Confucian Classic of Filial Piety (Xiaojing), and Solon's laws, which reportedly allowed prosecution for failure to support elderly parents. -- Aristotle, Nicomachean Ethics VIII.14 (1163b15-25), discusses the degree to which children owe their parents.

narrative:
"But if, when they are still more advanced in years, they continue the same neglect of their parents, and do any hurt to any of them, let them be brought before a court in which every single one of the eldest citizens shall be the judges, and if the offender be convicted, let the court determine what he ought to pay or suffer, and any penalty may be imposed on him which a man can pay or suffer.

"If the person who has been wronged be unable to inform the magistrates, let any freeman who hears of his case inform, and if he do not, he shall be deemed base, and shall be liable to have a suit for damage brought against him by any one who likes.

"And if a slave inform, he shall receive freedom; and if he be the slave of the injurer or injured party, he shall be set free by the magistrates, or if he belong to any other citizen, the public shall pay a price on his behalf to the owner; and let the magistrates take heed that no one wrongs him out of revenge, because he has given information."
refashioned:
"If people who are past the age threshold for corporal punishment continue to neglect or harm their parents, they shall be brought before a court composed of the eldest citizens as judges. If convicted, the court may impose any penalty that a person can pay or endure -- there is no limit.

"If the wronged parent is unable to report to the magistrates, any freeman who learns of the situation must report it. If he fails to do so, he shall be considered base and shall be vulnerable to a damages suit brought by anyone who chooses.

"If a slave provides the information, he shall be freed. If the slave belongs to either the offender or the victim, the magistrates shall free him. If the slave belongs to a third party, the state shall pay the owner fair compensation. The magistrates must ensure that no one retaliates against the informant for having provided information."
elegant: Older offenders who harm their parents face a court of the eldest citizens with unlimited sentencing power. If the wronged parent cannot report, any freeman who learns of the abuse must inform the authorities or face a damages suit for moral cowardice. Slave informants are freed at public expense, and the magistrates must protect them from retaliation.
summary: Older offenders who neglect or harm parents face a court of the eldest citizens with unlimited penalty power. Bystanders who fail to report parental abuse are legally liable, and slave informants receive their freedom with protection from retaliation.
distilled: Parental abuse is judged by the eldest citizens with unlimited sentencing power, bystander reporting is mandatory, and slave informants are rewarded with freedom.
notes: The Athenian Stranger completes the laws on filial obligations. -- The court composed entirely of the eldest citizens is a special tribunal reserved for the gravest offenses -- its composition ensures that the judges have the most personal understanding of aging and parental vulnerability. -- The unlimited sentencing authority ('any penalty which a man can pay or suffer') is the most expansive judicial discretion in the entire Laws, reflecting the extreme gravity of parental abuse. -- The mandatory reporting obligation for freemen, backed by damages liability, creates a system of collective moral responsibility -- every citizen is a guardian of filial duty. -- The slave-informant provision parallels the treasure-trove laws in 11.2, offering freedom as incentive and suggesting a consistent policy of rewarding slaves who serve the public interest. -- The anti-retaliation protection for informants is a sophisticated procedural safeguard. -- Compare Solon's laws requiring adult children to maintain their elderly parents, with penalties for non-compliance (Plutarch, Life of Solon 22). -- The provision that slave informants receive freedom mirrors Athenian legal practice in certain types of public actions.

narrative:
"Cases in which one man injures another by poisons, and which prove fatal, have been already discussed; but about other cases in which a person intentionally and of malice harms another with meats, or drinks, or ointments, nothing has as yet been determined.

"For there are two kinds of poisons used among men, which cannot clearly be distinguished. There is the kind just now explicitly mentioned, which injures bodies by the use of other bodies according to a natural law; there is also another kind which persuades the more daring class that they can do injury by sorceries, and incantations, and magic knots, as they are termed, and makes others believe that they above all persons are injured by the powers of the magician.

"Now it is not easy to know the nature of all these things; nor if a man do know can he readily persuade others to believe him. And when men are disturbed in their minds at the sight of waxen images fixed either at their doors, or in a place where three ways meet, or on the sepulchres of parents, there is no use in trying to persuade them that they should despise all such things because they have no certain knowledge about them."
refashioned:
"We have already addressed cases where one person fatally poisons another. But we have not yet dealt with cases where a person intentionally and maliciously harms another through food, drink, or ointments in ways that are not fatal.

"There are two kinds of harmful substances used among people, and they are difficult to distinguish clearly. The first is the kind already mentioned: substances that injure bodies through other bodies according to natural physical laws. The second kind is different: it involves sorcery, incantations, and what are called 'magic knots,' which convince the more audacious that they can harm others through supernatural means, and make potential victims believe they are especially vulnerable to a magician's power.

"The true nature of these things is not easy to know, and even someone who does understand cannot easily persuade others. When people see wax figures placed at their doors, at crossroads, or on their parents' graves, and are disturbed by them, it is useless to try persuading them to dismiss such things on the grounds that nobody really knows whether they have any power."
elegant: Two kinds of poisoning must be distinguished: physical poisoning through natural substances, and supposed sorcery through incantations, magic knots, and wax images. The nature of the second kind is genuinely difficult to determine, and people who are frightened by magical practices at their doors, crossroads, or ancestral tombs cannot simply be told to ignore what they do not understand.
summary: The Athenian Stranger distinguishes two types of harm: physical poisoning by natural substances and supposed harm through sorcery and magic. He acknowledges that the nature of sorcery is genuinely uncertain and that people's fears of magical practices cannot simply be dismissed.
distilled: Physical poisoning and sorcery must be addressed by law separately, since the nature of magical harm is genuinely uncertain and popular fear of it cannot simply be dismissed.
notes: The Athenian Stranger introduces the laws on poisoning and sorcery. -- The distinction between natural and supernatural poisoning reflects actual Greek cultural anxiety about the boundary between medicine (pharmakon as remedy) and witchcraft (pharmakon as poison/spell). The word pharmakon encompassed both meanings. -- Wax images (keroplaston) placed at doors, crossroads, and tombs were a real practice in Greek curse magic (katadesmoi/defixiones). Archaeological evidence of curse tablets and binding spells is abundant throughout the Greek world. -- Crossroads (triodoi) were sacred to Hecate and associated with magical practices. -- Plato's remarkably balanced treatment -- neither dismissing sorcery as nonsense nor affirming it as real -- reflects a philosophical sophistication about the limits of knowledge. He acknowledges that fear is a genuine social problem regardless of whether the feared object is real. -- Compare Laws X (909b-910d), where the Athenian Stranger discusses impiety and unauthorized religious practices. -- The Hippocratic treatise On the Sacred Disease takes a more rationalist stance, arguing that epilepsy is not caused by divine or magical forces.

narrative:
"But we must have a law in two parts, concerning poisoning, in whichever of the two ways the attempt is made, and we must entreat, and exhort, and advise men not to have recourse to such practices, by which they scare the multitude out of their wits, as if they were children, compelling the legislator and the judge to heal the fears which the sorcerer arouses, and to tell them in the first place, that he who attempts to poison or enchant others knows not what he is doing, either as regards the body (unless he has a knowledge of medicine), or as regards his enchantments (unless he happens to be a prophet or diviner).

"Let the law, then, run as follows about poisoning or witchcraft: He who employs poison to do any injury, not fatal, to a man himself, or to his servants, or any injury, whether fatal or not, to his cattle or his bees, if he be a physician, and be convicted of poisoning, shall be punished with death; or if he be a private person, the court shall determine what he is to pay or suffer. But he who seems to be the sort of man who injures others by magic knots, or enchantments, or incantations, or any of the like practices, if he be a prophet or diviner, let him die; and if, not being a prophet, he be convicted of witchcraft, as in the previous case, let the court fix what he ought to pay or suffer."
refashioned:
"We need a two-part law covering poisoning in both its forms. We must also entreat, exhort, and advise people not to resort to such practices, which terrify the public as if they were children, forcing the legislator and judge to address the fears that sorcerers create. People should understand that those who attempt to poison or enchant others generally do not know what they are doing -- not regarding the body, unless they have medical training, and not regarding enchantments, unless they happen to be a genuine prophet or diviner.

"The law on poisoning and witchcraft is as follows. Anyone who uses poison to cause non-fatal injury to a person, his servants, or any injury, whether fatal or not, to his livestock or bees: if the offender is a physician convicted of poisoning, he shall be punished with death. If the offender is a private person, the court shall determine the appropriate penalty. As for anyone who appears to harm others through magic knots, enchantments, incantations, or similar practices: if the offender is a prophet or diviner, he shall die. If he is not a prophet but is convicted of witchcraft, the court shall determine the penalty."
elegant: A two-part law addresses both physical poisoning and sorcery. Physicians convicted of poisoning face death; laypeople face court-determined penalties. Prophets or diviners convicted of harmful sorcery face death; non-prophets convicted of witchcraft face court-determined penalties. The severity scales with the offender's expertise: those who should know better are punished more harshly.
summary: The law on poisoning and sorcery imposes death on physicians convicted of poisoning and on prophets convicted of harmful sorcery, as experts who should know better. Non-experts in either category face penalties determined by the court.
distilled: Expert poisoners and expert sorcerers face death, while non-experts in either category face court-determined penalties proportional to the harm caused.
notes: The Athenian Stranger states the law on poisoning and sorcery. -- The key principle is that expertise increases culpability: physicians who poison and prophets who curse are treated more severely than laypeople, because they abuse specialized knowledge. This parallels the consumer-protection laws in 11.6-7, where skilled sellers face harsher penalties than ordinary ones. -- The distinction between prophets/diviners and ordinary people practicing sorcery creates a two-tier system: professional practitioners of the sacred arts bear greater responsibility. -- The death penalty for physician-poisoners reflects the special trust placed in medical professionals -- a concern that echoes the Hippocratic Oath's injunction to 'do no harm.' -- The pragmatic note that most would-be sorcerers 'know not what they are doing' suggests Plato views much sorcery as ineffective -- but harmful to social order nonetheless. -- Compare Republic III (389b-d) on the analogy between doctors, rulers, and the legitimate use of deception.

narrative:
"When a man does another any injury by theft or violence, for the greater injury let him pay greater damages to the injured man, and less for the smaller injury; but in all cases, whatever the injury may have been, as much as will compensate the loss. And besides the compensation of the wrong, let a man pay a further penalty for the chastisement of his offence: he who has done the wrong instigated by the folly of another, through the lightheartedness of youth or the like, shall pay a lighter penalty; but he who has injured another through his own folly, when overcome by pleasure or pain, in cowardly fear, or lust, or envy, or implacable anger, shall endure a heavier punishment.

"Not that he is punished because he did wrong, for that which is done can never be undone, but in order that in future times, he, and those who see him corrected, may utterly hate injustice, or at any rate abate much of their evil-doing.

"Having an eye to all these things, the law, like a good archer, should aim at the right measure of punishment, and in all cases at the deserved punishment. In the attainment of this the judge shall be a fellow-worker with the legislator, whenever the law leaves to him to determine what the offender shall suffer or pay; and the legislator, like a painter, shall give a rough sketch of the cases in which the law is to be applied.

"This is what we must do, Megillus and Cleinias, in the best and fairest manner that we can, saying what the punishments are to be of all actions of theft and violence, and giving laws of such a kind as the Gods and sons of Gods would have us give."
refashioned:
"When someone injures another through theft or violence, the damages should be proportional: greater damages for greater injuries, lesser for smaller ones, but in every case enough to fully compensate the loss. Beyond compensation, an additional penalty shall be imposed as corrective punishment. Those who acted under the influence of another's folly, or through youthful recklessness and the like, shall receive a lighter penalty. But those who acted from their own weakness -- overcome by pleasure or pain, cowardly fear, lust, envy, or implacable anger -- shall endure heavier punishment.

"The purpose of punishment is not retribution for the past, since what is done can never be undone. Rather, it is to ensure that in the future, both the offender and those who witness his correction come to hate injustice completely, or at the very least significantly reduce their wrongdoing.

"The law, like a skilled archer, should aim precisely at the right measure of punishment -- always the deserved penalty. In achieving this, the judge shall be the legislator's collaborator whenever the law leaves it to the judge to determine the specific penalty. The legislator, like a painter, provides the rough sketch; the judge fills in the details.

"This is what we must do, Megillus and Cleinias, as well and fairly as we can: specifying punishments for all acts of theft and violence, and crafting laws worthy of the gods and the sons of gods."
elegant: Damages for theft and violence must be proportional, fully compensating the loss plus an additional corrective penalty. Those influenced by others or by youth receive lighter penalties; those driven by their own lust, envy, fear, or anger receive heavier ones. Punishment is not retribution for the past but education for the future -- aimed at making offenders and witnesses hate injustice. The law, like a good archer, aims at the right measure; the legislator sketches, the judge fills in the details.
summary: Penalties for theft and violence must compensate the victim and correct the offender, scaled to the offender's moral culpability. Punishment is forward-looking -- not retribution but education for offender and community alike. The legislator provides the framework; the judge applies it to specifics.
distilled: Punishment for theft and violence serves not retribution but future deterrence, with the law aiming like a good archer at the proportional penalty each offender deserves.
notes: The Athenian Stranger addresses Megillus and Cleinias, stating the general theory of punishment. -- The forward-looking theory of punishment ('not because he did wrong, for that which is done can never be undone') is one of the Laws' most philosophically important statements, anticipating modern consequentialist and rehabilitative theories of criminal justice. -- The distinction between acting from one's own vice (lust, envy, anger) versus external influence or youth creates a graduated culpability scale anticipating modern distinctions between principal and accessory liability, and between adult and juvenile offenders. -- The archer metaphor for proportional punishment connects to the Laws' broader theme of measure and proportion as principles of good order. -- The painter-legislator analogy appears also in Republic VI (484c-d), where the philosopher-ruler is compared to a painter working from the model of the Forms. -- The invocation of 'Gods and sons of Gods' as the ultimate standard for legislation reflects the Laws' theocratic framework. -- Compare Protagoras 324a-c, where Socrates articulates a similar forward-looking theory of punishment.

narrative:
"If a man is mad he shall not be at large in the city, but his relations shall keep him at home in any way which they can; or if not, let them pay a penalty -- he who is of the highest class shall pay a penalty of one hundred drachmas, whether he be a slave or a freeman whom he neglects; and he of the second class shall pay four-fifths of a mina; and he of the third class three-fifths; and he of the fourth class two-fifths.

"Now there are many sorts of madness, some arising out of disease, which we have already mentioned; and there are other kinds, which originate in an evil and passionate temperament, and are increased by bad education; out of a slight quarrel this class of madmen will often raise a storm of abuse against one another, and nothing of that sort ought to be allowed to occur in a well-ordered state.

"Let this, then, be the law about abuse, which shall relate to all cases: No one shall speak evil of another; and when a man disputes with another he shall teach and learn of the disputant and the company, but he shall abstain from evil-speaking; for out of the imprecations which men utter against one another, and the feminine habit of casting aspersions on one another, and using foul names, out of words light as air, in very deed the greatest enmities and hatreds spring up."
refashioned:
"A person who is mad shall not roam freely in the city. His relatives must keep him confined at home by whatever means they can. If they fail to do so, they face penalties scaled to their property class: the highest class pays one hundred drachmas, the second class eighty drachmas, the third class sixty, and the fourth class forty -- regardless of whether the person they neglect is a slave or a free person.

"There are many forms of madness. Some arise from disease, which we have already discussed. But other kinds originate in an evil and passionate temperament, made worse by poor upbringing. This second type will escalate a trivial disagreement into a storm of verbal abuse -- and nothing of the sort should be tolerated in a well-ordered state.

"Here, then, is the law on verbal abuse, applying to all cases: no one shall speak ill of another. When people disagree, they should seek to teach and learn from the other party and from those present, but they must refrain from abusive language. For from the curses people hurl at each other, from the habit of casting aspersions and using foul names -- from words as light as air -- the greatest and most serious enmities and hatreds arise."
elegant: The insane must be confined at home by their relatives, with penalties scaled by property class for failure. Madness comes in two forms: from disease and from passionate temperament worsened by bad education. The second type fuels abusive speech, which the law bans entirely. Disputes must aim at mutual instruction, not insult, for from words light as air the greatest hatreds arise.
summary: Relatives must confine the insane at home or face scaled fines. The law then pivots to verbal abuse, which it treats as a form of madness: all evil-speaking is banned, because disputes must aim at mutual learning, not insult, since even light words can generate profound enmity.
distilled: Madness must be confined and abusive speech banned, because verbal insults -- though light as air -- generate the greatest enmities.
notes: The Athenian Stranger addresses both literal madness and the 'madness' of abusive speech. -- The scaled penalties by property class ensure proportional economic impact, a principle used throughout the Laws. One mina equaled 100 drachmas. -- The remarkable transition from clinical madness to the 'madness' of passionate speech suggests that Plato sees verbal abuse as a form of social insanity. -- The rule that disputants should 'teach and learn' rather than abuse each other reflects the Socratic method: proper disagreement is a form of collaborative inquiry. -- The phrase 'words light as air' generating 'the greatest enmities' anticipates modern understanding of how verbal aggression escalates into physical violence. -- The 'feminine habit' of casting aspersions reflects misogynistic Greek cultural assumptions, but the law itself applies equally to men and women. -- Compare Republic V (452a-e) on the importance of rational discourse over mockery. -- Aristotle's Rhetoric II.2-3 provides a detailed analysis of anger and its provocations that complements this passage.

narrative:
"For the speaker gratifies his anger, which is an ungracious element of his nature; and nursing up his wrath by the entertainment of evil thoughts, and exacerbating that part of his soul which was formerly civilized by education, he lives in a state of savageness and moroseness, and pays a bitter penalty for his anger.

"And in such cases almost all men take to saying something ridiculous about their opponent, and there is no man who is in the habit of laughing at another who does not miss virtue and earnestness altogether, or lose the better half of greatness.

"Wherefore let no one utter any taunting word at a temple, or at the public sacrifices, or at games, or in the agora, or in a court of justice, or in any public assembly. And let the magistrate who presides on these occasions chastise an offender, and he shall be blameless; but if he fails in doing so, he shall not claim the prize of virtue; for he is one who heeds not the laws, and does not do what the legislator commands."
refashioned:
"The speaker who indulges in abuse is gratifying his anger -- an ugly part of his nature. By nursing his rage through dwelling on malicious thoughts, he inflames the very part of his soul that education had previously civilized. He sinks into a condition of savagery and bitterness, paying a heavy price for his indulgence.

"In such situations, nearly everyone resorts to mocking their opponent. But no person who habitually laughs at others can achieve true virtue and seriousness -- or at the very least, such a person loses the better half of greatness.

"Therefore, no one shall utter taunting words at a temple, during public sacrifices, at athletic games, in the marketplace, in a court of law, or in any public assembly. The presiding magistrate must punish any offender and shall face no blame for doing so. But a magistrate who fails to enforce this rule forfeits his claim to virtue, for he has disregarded the laws and refused to carry out the legislator's command."
elegant: Verbal abuse gratifies anger but destroys the soul: it reverses education's civilizing work and reduces a person to savagery. Habitual mockery destroys virtue and seriousness. Taunting is therefore banned in all public spaces -- temples, sacrifices, games, courts, and assemblies. Presiding magistrates must punish offenders or forfeit their own claim to virtue.
summary: Verbal abuse degrades the speaker's soul by reversing education's civilizing influence and fostering savagery. Habitual mockery is incompatible with virtue. Taunting is banned in all public spaces, and presiding magistrates must enforce the ban or be considered negligent.
distilled: Verbal abuse destroys the abuser's soul by reversing the civilizing work of education, and is therefore banned in all public spaces with mandatory enforcement by magistrates.
notes: The Athenian Stranger provides the psychological rationale for banning verbal abuse. -- The concept that anger 're-savages' the soul, undoing what education civilized, is a powerful psychological insight connecting to the tripartite soul theory of Republic IV: the spirited element (thumos), when not governed by reason, regresses to a pre-civilized state. -- The observation that habitual mockery undermines virtue and 'the better half of greatness' reflects Plato's longstanding critique of comedy and ridicule; compare Republic X (606c), where Socrates argues that laughing at comedy weakens our moral resistance to base behavior. -- The comprehensive list of public spaces (temple, sacrifice, games, agora, court, assembly) covers every dimension of Greek public life, leaving no venue for verbal abuse. -- The duty imposed on presiding magistrates, with the penalty of losing their claim to virtue, makes enforcement a test of the magistrate's own character. -- Compare Laws VII (816d-817e) on the regulation of dramatic performances, and Republic III (388e-389a) on the dangers of uncontrolled laughter.

narrative:
"And if in any other place any one indulges in these sort of revilings, whether he has begun the quarrel or is only retaliating, let any elder who is present support the law, and control with blows those who indulge in passion, which is another great evil; and if he do not, let him be liable to pay the appointed penalty.

"And we say now, that he who deals in reproaches against others cannot reproach them without attempting to ridicule them; and this, when done in a moment of anger, is what we make matter of reproach against him. But then, do we admit into our state the comic writers who are so fond of making mankind ridiculous, if they attempt in a good-natured manner to turn the laugh against our citizens? Or do we draw the distinction of jest and earnest, and allow a man to make use of ridicule in jest and without anger about any thing or person; though as we were saying, not if he be angry and have a set purpose?

"We forbid earnest -- that is unalterably fixed; but we have still to say who are to be sanctioned or not to be sanctioned by the law in the employment of innocent humour. A comic poet, or maker of iambic or satirical lyric verse, shall not be permitted to ridicule any of the citizens, either by word or likeness, either in anger or without anger. And if any one is disobedient, the judges shall either at once expel him from the country, or he shall pay a fine of three minas, which shall be dedicated to the God who presides over the contests.

"Those only who have received permission shall be allowed to write verses at one another, but they shall be without anger and in jest; in anger and in serious earnest they shall not be allowed. The decision of this matter shall be left to the superintendent of the general education of the young, and whatever he may license, the writer shall be allowed to produce, and whatever he rejects let not the poet himself exhibit, or ever teach anybody else, slave or freeman, under the penalty of being dishonoured, and held disobedient to the laws."
refashioned:
"If anyone indulges in abusive speech anywhere else -- whether as the instigator or in retaliation -- any elder present must uphold the law and physically restrain those carried away by passion, which is itself a grave evil. An elder who fails to act shall be liable for the prescribed penalty.

"We maintain that a person who reproaches others inevitably tries to ridicule them. When done in anger, this is precisely what we condemn. But what about comic writers who good-naturedly try to make citizens laugh at themselves? Do we admit them into the state? Can we distinguish jest from earnest, allowing harmless ridicule made without anger while prohibiting malicious mockery?

"Our position is firm: angry, serious ridicule is absolutely forbidden. As for innocent humor, the rules are as follows. No comic poet, iambic writer, or satirical lyricist may ridicule any citizen, either in words or in caricature, whether in anger or in jest. Violators face immediate expulsion from the country or a fine of three minas dedicated to the god of the contests.

"Only those who have received explicit permission may compose verses about one another, and these must be without anger and purely in jest -- never in anger or serious earnest. The superintendent of youth education shall decide what is permissible. Whatever he approves may be performed; whatever he rejects, the poet may neither exhibit nor teach to anyone, slave or free, on pain of dishonor and conviction for disobedience to the laws."
elegant: If anyone indulges in abusive speech anywhere outside official public spaces, any elder present must restrain them with blows or face the appointed penalty. Reproach inevitably involves ridicule, which in anger is condemned. But what of comic writers who good-naturedly mock citizens? The state draws a clear line: angry, serious ridicule is absolutely forbidden. No comic poet, iambic writer, or satirical lyricist may ridicule any citizen, by word or likeness, in anger or otherwise. Violators face immediate expulsion or a fine of three minas dedicated to the god of the contests. Only those who receive explicit permission from the superintendent of youth education may compose humorous verse about fellow citizens, and this must be purely in jest, never in anger or earnest. Whatever the superintendent rejects, the poet may not exhibit or teach to anyone, slave or free, on pain of dishonor and disobedience to the laws.
summary: Elders must enforce the abuse ban by physical restraint. Comic poets and satirists are prohibited from ridiculing citizens in any form. Only verse explicitly licensed by the superintendent of youth education may be composed about fellow citizens, and it must be purely jesting, never angry or serious.
distilled: Comic poets may not ridicule citizens without explicit permission from the superintendent of education, and all angry or serious mockery is absolutely forbidden.
notes: The Athenian Stranger extends the abuse ban to literary satire. -- This is one of the most discussed passages in the Laws for its implications regarding censorship and freedom of expression. It extends the Republic's famous censorship of poetry (Books II-III, X) into the domain of comedy and satire. -- The three-mina fine is substantial -- roughly equivalent to 300 days' wages for a skilled laborer. -- The distinction between jest and earnest is philosophically interesting but practically dissolved by the law's prohibition of citizen-directed ridicule even in jest. -- The superintendent of youth education (who appears in Laws VII as well) serves as a state censor -- a role Plato considers essential for maintaining civic virtue. -- Aristophanes, the great Athenian comic poet, regularly ridiculed prominent citizens; his mockery of Socrates in the Clouds is the most famous example and may have contributed to the prejudice that led to Socrates' trial. -- The passage may reflect Plato's belief that such mockery was genuinely harmful to individuals and to civic life. -- Compare Republic III (394-398), where poetic mimesis is restricted to representations of virtuous characters.

narrative:
"Now he is not to be pitied who is hungry, or who suffers any bodily pain, but he who is temperate, or has some other virtue, or part of a virtue, and at the same time suffers from misfortune; it would be an extraordinary thing if such an one, whether slave or freeman, were utterly forsaken and fell into the extremes of poverty in any tolerably well-ordered city or government.

"Wherefore the legislator may safely make a law applicable to such cases in the following terms: Let there be no beggars in our state; and if anybody begs, seeking to pick up a livelihood by unavailing prayers, let the wardens of the agora turn him out of the agora, and the wardens of the city out of the city, and the wardens of the country send him out of any other parts of the land across the border, in order that the land may be cleared of this sort of animal."
refashioned:
"The person truly deserving of pity is not the one who is hungry or in physical pain, but the one who possesses temperance or some other virtue -- or even part of a virtue -- yet suffers misfortune despite it. In any tolerably well-ordered city, it would be extraordinary if such a virtuous person, whether slave or free, were completely abandoned and fell into extreme poverty.

"The legislator can therefore safely enact the following law: there shall be no beggars in our state. If anyone attempts to beg, seeking a livelihood through futile appeals, the wardens of the agora shall expel him from the marketplace, the wardens of the city from the city, and the wardens of the country from the countryside and across the border. The land shall be cleared of this sort of creature."
elegant: True pity belongs to the virtuous person struck by misfortune, not to anyone who merely suffers hunger or pain. In a well-ordered state, virtuous people should never fall into destitution. Begging is therefore banned: beggars shall be expelled from agora, city, and countryside alike, driven beyond the borders.
summary: The Athenian Stranger argues that a well-ordered state should never allow virtuous people to fall into destitution, making begging unnecessary. Begging is therefore banned outright, with beggars to be expelled from all parts of the state by the appropriate wardens.
distilled: In a well-ordered state, no virtuous person should fall into destitution, so begging is banned and beggars are expelled across the border.
notes: The Athenian Stranger addresses poverty and begging. -- This passage reveals a troubling logic: the ban on begging assumes that a well-ordered state provides for all virtuous citizens, so anyone who begs must be either lacking virtue or gaming the system. -- The harsh language ('cleared of this sort of animal') is striking and reflects Greek attitudes toward those considered social parasites. -- The assumption that the state can eliminate poverty through good governance is idealistic; the Laws elsewhere acknowledges that the property system cannot prevent all inequality. -- Compare Republic II (369b-374a), where the ideal city is designed to meet all citizens' needs through the division of labor, theoretically eliminating poverty. -- The distinction between deserving and undeserving poor has a long history in Western political thought, from this passage through the Elizabethan Poor Laws to modern welfare debates. -- The three-warden enforcement system (agora, city, country) parallels the enforcement of marketplace regulations in 11.9-10.

narrative:
"If a slave of either sex injure anything, which is not his or her own, through inexperience, or some improper practice, and the person who suffers damage be not himself in part to blame, the master of the slave who has done the harm shall either make full satisfaction, or give up the slave who has done the injury.

"But if the master argue that the charge has arisen by collusion between the injured party and the injurer, with the view of obtaining the slave, let him sue the person who says that he has been injured, for malpractices. And if he gain a conviction, let him receive double the value which the court fixes as the price of the slave; and if he lose his suit, let him make amends for the injury, and give up the slave.

"And if a beast of burden, or horse, or dog, or any other animal, injure the property of a neighbour, the owner shall in like manner pay for the injury."
refashioned:
"If a slave of either sex damages someone else's property through inexperience or carelessness, and the injured party is not partly at fault, the slave's master must either make full compensation or surrender the slave who caused the damage.

"However, if the master claims the charge is based on collusion between the injured party and the slave, aimed at fraudulently obtaining the slave, the master may sue the supposed victim for fraud. If the master wins, he shall receive double the court-assessed value of the slave. If he loses, he must compensate for the damage and surrender the slave.

"The same principle applies to animals: if a beast of burden, horse, dog, or any other animal damages a neighbor's property, the owner shall pay for the injury in the same manner."
elegant: When a slave damages another's property without the victim's contributory fault, the master must either pay full compensation or surrender the slave. If the master suspects the claim is a fraudulent scheme to acquire the slave, he may countersue; winning earns double the slave's value, losing means paying damages and surrendering the slave. The same principle applies to damage caused by animals.
summary: Masters are liable for damage caused by their slaves through either compensation or surrender of the slave. A defense of collusion is available, with double damages for a successful countersuit. The same liability framework applies to damage caused by animals.
distilled: Masters bear strict liability for damage caused by their slaves or animals, with an option to surrender the offending slave or pay full compensation.
notes: The Athenian Stranger addresses liability for slaves and animals. -- The choice between compensation and surrender of the slave (noxal surrender) is one of the most ancient principles of property law, found in the Twelve Tables of Roman law (451-450 BCE) and in Near Eastern legal codes before that. -- The contributory-fault defense ('the person who suffers damage be not himself in part to blame') is a remarkably early expression of comparative negligence. -- The collusion defense protects masters against fraudulent schemes to acquire their slaves through staged accidents -- apparently a recognized problem in the ancient world. -- The extension of the principle to animals (horses, dogs, beasts of burden) establishes a unified framework of owner liability for property under one's control. -- Compare Exodus 21:28-36, which contains similar provisions for liability when an ox gores a person or another animal. -- Roman law developed noxal surrender (noxae deditio) into a sophisticated legal institution, allowing masters to avoid personal liability by surrendering the offending slave.

narrative:
"If any man refuses to be a witness, he who wants him shall summon him, and he who is summoned shall come to the trial; and if he knows and is willing to bear witness, let him bear witness, but if he says he does not know let him swear by the three divinities Zeus, and Apollo, and Themis, that he does not, and have no more to do with the cause.

"And he who is summoned to give witness and does not answer to his summoner, shall be liable for the harm which ensues according to law. And if a person calls up as a witness any one who is acting as a judge, let him give his witness, but he shall not afterwards vote in the cause.

"A free woman may give her witness and plead, if she be more than forty years of age, and may bring an action if she have no husband; but if her husband be alive she shall only be allowed to bear witness. A slave of either sex and a child shall be allowed to give evidence and to plead, but only in cases of murder; and they must produce sufficient sureties that they will certainly remain until the trial, in case they should be charged with false witness.

"And either of the parties in a cause may bring an accusation of perjury against witnesses, touching their evidence in whole or in part, if he asserts that such evidence has been given; but the accusation must be brought previous to the final decision of the cause."
refashioned:
"If someone refuses to serve as a witness, the party who needs his testimony shall issue a formal summons. The summoned person must appear at trial. If he knows the relevant facts and is willing to testify, he shall testify. But if he claims to have no knowledge, he must swear by the three deities -- Zeus, Apollo, and Themis -- that he does not, and then he shall be released from any further involvement in the case.

"Anyone summoned as a witness who fails to appear shall be legally liable for whatever harm results. If a sitting judge is called as a witness, he shall give his testimony but may not subsequently vote in the case.

"A free woman over forty years of age may testify and plead in court. She may also bring legal actions, but only if she has no living husband. If her husband is alive, she may only serve as a witness. Slaves of either sex and children may give evidence and plead, but only in murder cases, and they must provide sufficient sureties guaranteeing they will remain available through the trial in case they are charged with giving false testimony.

"Either party in a case may accuse witnesses of perjury, whether regarding all or part of their testimony. However, the perjury accusation must be filed before the final judgment in the case is rendered."
elegant: Summoned witnesses must appear at trial. Those who claim ignorance must swear by Zeus, Apollo, and Themis. Absent witnesses are liable for resulting harm. Judges called as witnesses must testify but cannot vote. Free women over forty may testify and, if unmarried, bring legal actions. Slaves and children may testify only in murder cases, with sureties required. Perjury accusations must be filed before final judgment.
summary: Witnesses are compelled to appear and testify, with a divine oath available for those claiming ignorance. Judges may testify but not vote. Women over forty gain limited legal standing, and slaves and children may testify only in murder cases. Perjury charges must precede the final verdict.
distilled: The law compels witness attendance, provides for oath-based refusal, grants limited legal standing to women over forty and to slaves in murder cases, and allows pre-verdict perjury challenges.
notes: The Athenian Stranger addresses witness law. -- The oath by Zeus, Apollo, and Themis invokes the three deities most associated with truth and justice: Zeus as supreme authority, Apollo as god of prophecy and truth, and Themis as goddess of divine law and customary right. -- The recusal of judge-witnesses from voting is an early conflict-of-interest provision. -- The age threshold of forty for women's legal standing is notable: it grants older women significant legal capacity (testimony, pleading, bringing actions) while restricting married women to testimony only. This is more liberal than Athenian practice, where women had very limited legal standing and usually needed a male representative (kyrios). -- The restriction of slave and child testimony to murder cases reflects both the gravity of such cases and the general suspicion of testimony from low-status persons. -- The pre-verdict perjury requirement ensures that false witness claims are resolved before they can affect the outcome. -- Compare Demosthenes, Against Stephanus, for extended discussion of witness law in classical Athens.

narrative:
"The magistrates shall preserve the accusations of false witness, and have them kept under the seal of both parties, and produce them on the day when the trial for false witness takes place. If a man be twice convicted of false witness, he shall not be required, and if thrice, he shall not be allowed to bear witness; and if he dare to witness after he has been convicted three times, let any one who pleases inform against him to the magistrates, and let the magistrates hand him over to the court, and if he be convicted he shall be punished with death.

"And in any case in which the evidence is rightly found to be false, and yet to have given the victory to him who wins the suit, and more than half the witnesses are condemned, the decision which was gained by these means shall be reconsidered, and there shall be a discussion and a decision as to whether the suit was determined by that false evidence or not; and in whichever way the decision may be given, the previous suit shall be determined accordingly."
refashioned:
"The magistrates shall preserve all accusations of false witness under the seal of both parties and present them on the day the perjury trial takes place. If a person is convicted of false witness twice, he shall no longer be required to testify. If convicted three times, he shall be forbidden from testifying at all. If he dares to give testimony after three convictions, anyone may report him to the magistrates, who shall hand him over to the court. If convicted again, he shall be punished with death.

"Furthermore, if evidence is correctly determined to be false and that false evidence gave the winning party his victory, and more than half the witnesses in the case are convicted of perjury, the original verdict shall be reopened. A new proceeding shall determine whether the false testimony actually decided the outcome. The original case shall then be resolved according to that finding."
elegant: False witness accusations are preserved under seal for trial. Two perjury convictions exempt a person from compulsory testimony; three convictions ban testimony entirely, with death for defiance. When false testimony is found to have decided a case and more than half the witnesses are convicted, the original verdict is reopened and re-decided based on whether the perjury actually determined the outcome.
summary: The law creates an escalating system for perjurers: two convictions exempt from compulsory testimony, three convictions ban testimony entirely, and further violation brings death. When perjured testimony is found to have determined a verdict, the original case is reopened.
distilled: Perjurers face escalating sanctions from exemption to ban to death, and verdicts obtained through proven false testimony are subject to reopening.
notes: The Athenian Stranger completes the laws on testimony and perjury. -- The escalating sanctions (exemption, ban, death) create a progressive system that permanently removes habitual perjurers from the judicial process. -- The sealed preservation of perjury accusations is a procedural safeguard ensuring that evidence is preserved for the separate perjury trial. -- The provision for reopening verdicts obtained through perjured testimony is a remarkable early form of what modern law calls 'fraud on the court' -- the principle that a judgment obtained through fraud is not truly final. -- The requirement that more than half the witnesses be convicted before reopening shows a high threshold for disturbing settled judgments, balancing finality against justice. -- Athenian law took perjury seriously: the graphe pseudomarturion (public action for false witness) was a common legal proceeding, and Demosthenes' speeches Against Stephanus are devoted to false witness claims. -- The death penalty for testifying after three perjury convictions reflects the view that chronic dishonesty in judicial proceedings is a fundamental threat to the legal order.

narrative:
"There are many noble things in human life, but to most of them attach evils which are fated to corrupt and spoil them. Is not justice noble, which has been the civilizer of humanity? How then can the advocate of justice be other than noble? And yet upon this profession which is presented to us under the fair name of art has come an evil reputation.

"In the first place, we are told that by ingenious pleas and the help of an advocate the law enables a man to win a particular cause, whether just or unjust; and the power of speech which is thereby imparted, are at the service of him who is willing to pay for them. Now in our state this so-called art, whether really an art or only an experience and practice destitute of any art, ought if possible never to come into existence, or if existing among us should listen to the request of the legislator and go away into another land, and not speak contrary to justice.

"If the offenders obey we say no more; but those who disobey, the voice of the law is as follows: If anyone thinks that he will pervert the power of justice in the minds of the judges, and unseasonably litigate or advocate, let any one who likes indict him for malpractices of law and dishonest advocacy, and let him be judged in the court of select judges; and if he be convicted, let the court determine whether he may be supposed to act from a love of money or from contentiousness.

"And if he is supposed to act from contentiousness, the court shall fix a time during which he shall not be allowed to institute or plead a cause; and if he is supposed to act as he does from love of money, in case he be a stranger, he shall leave the country, and never return under penalty of death; but if he be a citizen, he shall die, because he is a lover of money, in whatever manner gained; and equally, if he be judged to have acted more than once from contentiousness, he shall die."
refashioned:
"There are many noble things in human life, but most of them attract evils that corrupt and ruin them. Is not justice noble -- the very thing that has civilized humanity? How then can the advocate of justice be anything but noble? Yet this profession, which presents itself under the fair name of an art, has acquired a terrible reputation.

"We are told that through clever arguments and the help of advocates, the law enables people to win cases whether their cause is just or unjust, and that this power of persuasive speech is available to whoever can pay for it. In our state, this so-called art -- whether it truly deserves to be called an art or is merely a knack acquired through practice -- should ideally never come into existence. If it does exist among us, it should heed the legislator's request and take itself to another country rather than speak against justice.

"If practitioners comply, nothing more need be said. But for those who disobey, the law speaks as follows: anyone who attempts to pervert the judges' understanding of justice, or who litigates or advocates vexatiously, may be indicted by any citizen for legal malpractice and dishonest advocacy before the court of select judges. If convicted, the court shall determine whether the offender acted from love of money or from mere contentiousness.

"If the motive is contentiousness, the court shall bar him from initiating or pleading cases for a fixed period. If the motive is love of money: a foreigner shall be permanently expelled and face death if he returns; a citizen shall be put to death, because he is a lover of money in whatever form. And if anyone is found to have acted from contentiousness more than once, he too shall die."
elegant: Justice has civilized humanity, yet advocacy -- which should serve justice -- has been corrupted into a tool for winning cases regardless of merit, available to the highest bidder. Ideally, this corrupt practice should not exist; failing that, it should be expelled. Those who pervert justice through vexatious litigation or dishonest advocacy face indictment. The contentious are temporarily barred from the courts. Money-motivated advocates face death (citizens) or permanent banishment under penalty of death (foreigners). Repeat contentiousness also brings death.
summary: Book 11 concludes with a fierce attack on corrupt advocacy. While justice itself is noble, the legal profession has been corrupted by serving anyone who can pay. The law bans vexatious litigation and dishonest advocacy, with penalties ranging from temporary court bans to death depending on whether the motive is contentiousness or greed.
distilled: Corrupt legal advocacy -- which perverts justice by serving the highest bidder -- is punishable by temporary court bans for the contentious and death for the money-motivated.
notes: The Athenian Stranger concludes Book 11 with a critique of legal advocacy. -- The observation that noble things attract corrupting evils is a recurring Platonic theme; compare Republic VI (490d-495b) on how philosophy attracts charlatans who corrupt it. -- The question whether advocacy is a true 'art' (techne) or merely an 'experience' (empeiria) echoes the Gorgias (462b-466a), where Socrates distinguishes genuine arts from flattering knacks, classifying rhetoric as the latter. -- The invitation for corrupt advocacy to 'go away into another land' is darkly humorous -- Plato offers the profession a chance to leave voluntarily before imposing legal sanctions. -- The death penalty for money-motivated citizens is the harshest penalty in Book 11, reflecting Plato's view that selling justice for profit is among the gravest possible offenses against the state. -- The distinction between contentiousness (philoneikia) and love of money (philochrematia) as motives for corrupt advocacy shows psychological sophistication in sentencing. -- Compare Apology 31c-32a, where Socrates explains why he avoided public advocacy. -- The passage is also relevant to the Sophist, where Plato distinguishes genuine from false practitioners of intellectual arts.
